quote:
1. Manopubbaṅgamā dhammā, manoseṭṭhā manomayā;
All phenomena are preceded by the mind, created by the mind, and have the mind as their master.
Manasā ce paduṭṭhena, bhāsati vā karoti vā;
If one speaks or acts from a corrupted mind,
Tato naṃ dukkhamanveti, cakkaṃva vahato padaṃ.
suffering follows as the cart-wheel follows the ox’s hoof.

2. Manopubbaṅgamā dhammā, manoseṭṭhā manomayā;
All phenomena are preceded by the mind, created by the mind, and have the mind as their master.
Manasā ce pasannena, bhāsati vā karoti vā;
If one speaks or acts with a pure mind,
Tato naṃ sukhamanveti, chāyāva anapāyinī.
happiness follows as an ever-present shadow.

3. Akkocchi maṃ avadhi maṃ, ajini maṃ ahāsi me;
He insulted me; he struck me; he defeated me; he robbed me.
Ye ca taṃ upanayhanti, veraṃ tesaṃ na sammati.
For those who dwell on such resentments, enmity never ceases.

4. Akkocchi maṃ avadhi maṃ, ajini maṃ ahāsi me;
He insulted me; he struck me; he defeated me; he robbed me.
Ye ca taṃ nupanayhanti, veraṃ tesūpasammati.
For those who do not dwell on such resentments, enmity subsides.

5. Na hi verena verāni, sammantīdha kudācanaṃ;
For enmities are never appeased by enmity here at any time.
Averena ca sammanti, esa dhammo sanantano.
They are appeased by peace; this is an eternal law.

6. Pare ca na vijānanti, mayamettha yamāmase;
And they do not realize that we all must die here.
Ye ca tattha vijānanti, tato sammanti medhagā.
Those who do realize this, their quarrels appease.

7. Subhānupassiṃ viharantaṃ, indriyesu asaṃvutaṃ;
One who lives seeing pleasant things, unrestrained in the faculties,
Bhojanamhi cāmattaññuṃ, kusītaṃ hīnavīriyaṃ;
immoderate in food, listless and lazy,
Taṃ ve pasahati māro, vāto rukkhaṃva dubbalaṃ.
Māra overcomes him, just as wind overcomes a weak tree.

8. Asubhānupassiṃ viharantaṃ, indriyesu susaṃvutaṃ;
One who lives seeing unpleasant things, well-restrained in the faculties,
Bhojanamhi ca mattaññuṃ, saddhaṃ āraddhavīriyaṃ;
moderate in food, possessed of faith and energy,
Taṃ ve nappasahati māro, vāto selaṃva pabbataṃ.
Māra cannot overcome him, just as wind cannot overcome a rocky mountain.

9. Anikkasāvo kāsāvaṃ, yo vatthaṃ paridahissati;
Whoever would wear the yellow robe while still hampered by defilement,
Apeto damasaccena, na so kāsāvamarahati.
lacking in self-control and truth, is unworthy of that yellow robe.

10. Yo ca vantakasāvassa, sīlesu susamāhito;
But whoever has thrown off defilement, is well-established in virtue,
Upeto damasaccena, sa ve kāsāvamarahati.
possessed of self-control and truth, he is truly worthy of the yellow robe.

11. Asāre sāramatino, sāre cāsāradassino;
Those who deem the worthless as valuable, and see the valuable as worthless,
Te sāraṃ nādhigacchanti, micchāsaṅkappagocarā.
they do not attain the valuable, roaming in the field of wrong thought.

12. Sārañca sārato ñatvā, asārañca asārato;
But those who have known the valuable as valuable, and the worthless as worthless,
Te sāraṃ adhigacchanti, sammāsaṅkappagocarā.
they attain the valuable, roaming in the field of right thought.

13. Yathā agāraṃ ducchannaṃ, vuṭṭhī samativijjhati;
Just as rain penetrates a poorly thatched house,
Evaṃ abhāvitaṃ cittaṃ, rāgo samativijjhati.
so lust penetrates an undeveloped mind.

14. Yathā agāraṃ suchannaṃ, vuṭṭhī na samativijjhati;
Just as rain does not penetrate a well-thatched house,
Evaṃ subhāvitaṃ cittaṃ, rāgo na samativijjhati.
so lust does not penetrate a well-developed mind.

15. Idha socati pecca socati, pāpakārī ubhayattha socati;
Here he grieves, after death he grieves; the evil-doer grieves in both states.
So socati so vihaññati, disvā kammakiliṭṭhamattano.
He grieves, he is tormented, having seen his own defiled deeds.

16. Idha modati pecca modati, katapuñño ubhayattha modati;
Here he rejoices, after death he rejoices; he who has done merit rejoices in both states.
So modati so pamodati, disvā kammavisuddhimattano.
He rejoices, he is greatly happy, having seen his own pure deeds.

17. Idha tappati pecca tappati, pāpakārī ubhayattha tappati;
Here he is tormented, after death he is tormented; the evil-doer is tormented in both states.
‘‘ Pāpaṃ me katan ’’ ti tappati, bhiyyo tappati duggatiṃ gato.
He is tormented knowing, “I have done evil”; having gone to a bad destination, he is tormented still more.

18. Idha nandati pecca nandati, katapuñño ubhayattha nandati;
Here he rejoices, after death he rejoices; he who has done merit rejoices in both states.
‘‘ Puññaṃ me katan ’’ ti nandati, bhiyyo nandati suggatiṃ gato.
He rejoices knowing, “I have done merit”; having gone to a good destination, he rejoices still more.

19. Bahumpi ce saṃhita bhāsamāno, na takkaro hoti naro pamatto;
Even if one recites much scripture, the heedless person does not act accordingly,
Gopova gāvo gaṇayaṃ paresaṃ, na bhāgavā sāmaññassa hoti.
like a cowherd counting others' cows, he does not share in the fruits of monastic life.

20. Appampi ce saṃhita bhāsamāno, dhammassa hoti anudhammacārī;
Even if one recites little scripture, but lives in conformity with the Teaching,
Rāgañca dosañca pahāya mohaṃ, sammappajāno suvimuttacitto;
having abandoned lust, ill will, and delusion, with right knowledge and a well-liberated mind,
Anupādiyāno idha vā huraṃ vā, sa bhāgavā sāmaññassa hoti.
not clinging here or hereafter, he shares in the fruits of monastic life.
Yamakavaggo paṭhamo niṭṭhito.
The first chapter, the Chapter of Pairs, is completed.

21. Appamādo amatapadaṃ, pamādo maccuno padaṃ;
Diligence is the path to the Deathless, negligence is the path to death;
Appamattā na mīyanti, ye pamattā yathā matā.
The diligent do not die; the negligent are as if already dead.

22. Evaṃ visesato ñatvā, appamādamhi paṇḍitā;
Having known this distinction, the wise in diligence;
Appamāde pamodanti, ariyānaṃ gocare ratā.
Rejoice in heedfulness, delighting in the field of the noble ones.

23. Te jhāyino sātatikā, niccaṃ daḷhaparakkamā;
Those meditative ones, persistent, always of steadfast exertion;
Phusanti dhīrā nibbānaṃ, yogakkhemaṃ anuttaraṃ.
The wise touch Nibbāna, the unsurpassed security from the bonds.

24. Uṭṭhānavato satīmato, sucikammassa nisammakārino;
For one who is energetic, mindful, pure in action, and careful,
Saññatassa dhammajīvino, appamattassa yasobhivaḍḍhati.
Restrained, living according to the Dhamma, and diligent, fame grows.

25. Uṭṭhānenappamādena, saṃyamena damena ca;
Through effort, diligence, restraint, and self-control,
Dīpaṃ kayirātha medhāvī, yaṃ ogho nābhikīrati.
The wise one should make an island that a flood cannot overwhelm.

26. Pamādamanuyuñjanti, bālā dummedhino janā;
The foolish and ignorant give themselves to negligence,
Appamādañca medhāvī, dhanaṃ seṭṭhaṃva rakkhati.
While the wise one guards diligence as the supreme treasure.

27. Mā pamādamanuyuñjetha, mā kāmaratisanthavaṃ;
Do not give yourself to negligence, nor to intimacy with sensual pleasure;
Appamatto hi jhāyanto, pappoti vipulaṃ sukhaṃ.
For the diligent, meditative person attains abundant happiness.

28. Pamādaṃ appamādena, yadā nudati paṇḍito;
When the wise one expels negligence by diligence,
Paññāpāsādamāruyha, asoko sokiniṃ pajaṃ;
Having ascended the stronghold of wisdom, sorrowless, he observes the sorrowing crowd;
Pabbataṭṭhova bhūmaṭṭhe, dhīro bāle avekkhati.
The wise one observes the foolish as one standing on a mountain observes those on the plain.

29. Appamatto pamattesu, suttesu bahujāgaro;
Diligent among the negligent, wide awake among the sleeping,
Abalassaṃva sīghasso, hitvā yāti sumedhaso.
The wise one moves on, like a swift horse leaving behind a weak nag.

30. Appamādena maghavā, devānaṃ seṭṭhataṃ gato;
By diligence, Maghavā (Indra) attained the status of being the best of the devas;
Appamādaṃ pasaṃsanti, pamādo garahito sadā.
Diligence is praised; negligence is always derided.

31. Appamādarato bhikkhu, pamāde bhayadassi vā;
The bhikkhu who delights in diligence, or who sees danger in negligence,
Saṃyojanaṃ aṇuṃ thūlaṃ, ḍahaṃ aggīva gacchati.
Goes forth like a fire burning every fetter, gross and subtle.

32. Appamādarato bhikkhu, pamāde bhayadassi vā;
The bhikkhu who delights in diligence, or who sees danger in negligence,
Abhabbo parihānāya, nibbānasseva santike.
Is incapable of decline, and is close to Nibbāna.
Appamādavaggo dutiyo niṭṭhito.
The second chapter, the Chapter on Heedfulness, is completed.

33. Phandanaṃ capalaṃ cittaṃ, dūrakkhaṃ dunnivārayaṃ;
The mind is wavering and unsteady, difficult to guard, hard to restrain.
Ujuṃ karoti medhāvī, usukārova tejanaṃ.
The wise one sets it straight, as a fletcher straightens the arrow’s shaft.

34. Vārijova thale khitto, okamokataubbhato;
Like a fish thrown on land from its home in the water,
Pariphandatidaṃ cittaṃ, māradheyyaṃ pahātave.
This mind thrashes to escape Māra’s realm.

35. Dunniggahassa lahuno, yatthakāmanipātino;
Difficult to control, swift, jumping toward what it desires,
Cittassa damatho sādhu, cittaṃ dantaṃ sukhāvahaṃ.
Good is the taming of the mind; the tamed mind brings happiness.

36. Sududdasaṃ sunipuṇaṃ, yatthakāmanipātinaṃ;
So difficult to perceive, so subtle, jumping at what it desires,
Cittaṃ rakkhetha medhāvī, cittaṃ guttaṃ sukhāvahaṃ.
The wise should guard the mind; the protected mind brings happiness.

37. Dūraṅgamaṃ ekacaraṃ, asarīraṃ guhāsayaṃ;
The mind wanders far and alone, incorporeal and resting in the cavern.
Ye cittaṃ saṃyamessanti, mokkhanti mārabandhanā.
Those who restrain it will be released from Māra’s bonds.

38. Anavaṭṭhitacittassa, saddhammaṃ avijānato;
Wisdom does not mature in one of unsteady mind,
Pariplavapasādassa, paññā na paripūrati.
And drifting faith, who knows not the true Dhamma.

39. Anavassutacittassa, ananvāhatacetaso;
For the one who is awake, whose mind is neither afflicted nor filled with desire,
Puññapāpapahīnassa, natthi jāgarato bhayaṃ.
Who has transcended merit and demerit, there is no fear.

40. Kumbhūpamaṃ kāyamimaṃ viditvā, nagarūpamaṃ cittamidaṃ ṭhapetvā;
Having understood this body as a clay pot, having established this mind as a fortress,
Yodhetha māraṃ paññāvudhena, jitañca rakkhe anivesano siyā.
One should battle Māra with the sword of wisdom, and protect what has been conquered, clinging to nothing.

41. Aciraṃ vatayaṃ kāyo, pathaviṃ adhisessati;
Soon indeed, this body will lie on the earth,
Chuddho apetaviññāṇo, niratthaṃva kaliṅgaraṃ.
Without consciousness, cast away as a worthless log.

42. Diso disaṃ yaṃ taṃ kayirā, verī vā pana verinaṃ;
Whatever an enemy would do to an enemy, or a hater to one hated,
Micchāpaṇihitaṃ cittaṃ, pāpiyo naṃ tato kare.
Worse than that is the harm that a wrongly placed mind can do to itself.

43. Na taṃ mātā pitā kayirā, aññe vāpi ca ñātakā;
Whatever mother, father, or other relative may do,
Sammāpaṇihitaṃ cittaṃ, seyyaso naṃ tato kare.
Better still, the good that a rightly placed mind can do for itself.
Cittavaggo tatiyo niṭṭhito.
The third chapter, the Chapter on the Mind, is concluded.

44. Ko imaṃ pathaviṃ vicessati, yamalokañca imaṃ sadevakaṃ;
Who will conquer this earth, and the realm of Yama, and this world with its gods?
Ko dhammapadaṃ sudesitaṃ, kusalo pupphamiva pacessati.
Who will select the well-taught path of Dhamma, as an expert selects a flower?

45. Sekho pathaviṃ vicessati, yamalokañca imaṃ sadevakaṃ;
A trainee will conquer this earth, and the realm of Yama, and this world with its gods.
Sekho dhammapadaṃ sudesitaṃ, kusalo pupphamiva pacessati.
A trainee will select the well-taught path of Dhamma, as an expert selects a flower.

46. Pheṇūpamaṃ kāyamimaṃ viditvā, marīcidhammaṃ abhisambudhāno;
Having understood this body to be like foam, realizing its nature like a mirage,
Chetvāna mārassa papupphakāni, adassanaṃ maccurājassa gacche.
Having cut off the flowers of Māra, one may go unseen by the King of Death.

47. Pupphāni heva pacinantaṃ, byāsattamanasaṃ naraṃ;
As for the individual who is only gathering flowers, with a mind clinging,
Suttaṃ gāmaṃ mahoghova, maccu ādāya gacchati.
Death carries him away, as a great flood carries away a sleeping village.

48. Pupphāni heva pacinantaṃ, byāsattamanasaṃ naraṃ;
As for the individual who is only gathering flowers, with a mind clinging,
Atittaññeva kāmesu, antako kurute vasaṃ.
Unsatisfied by sensual pleasures, the Ender brings him under his control.

49. Yathāpi bhamaro pupphaṃ, vaṇṇagandhamaheṭhayaṃ;
Just as a bee, without harming the flower, its color, or its fragrance,
Paleti rasamādāya, evaṃ gāme munī care.
Departs having taken the nectar, so should a sage go through a village.

50. Na paresaṃ vilomāni, na paresaṃ katākataṃ;
One should not attend to the wrongdoings of others, nor to what others have done or failed to do;
Attanova avekkheyya, katāni akatāni ca.
One should attend only to oneself, to what one has done and failed to do.

51. Yathāpi ruciraṃ pupphaṃ, vaṇṇavantaṃ agandhakaṃ;
Just as a beautiful flower that is full of color but lacks fragrance,
Evaṃ subhāsitā vācā, aphalā hoti akubbato.
So too is a well-spoken word fruitless for one who does not act.

52. Yathāpi ruciraṃ pupphaṃ, vaṇṇavantaṃ sugandhakaṃ;
Just as a beautiful flower that is full of color and fragrant,
Evaṃ subhāsitā vācā, saphalā hoti kubbato.
So too is a well-spoken word fruitful for one who acts.

53. Yathāpi puppharāsimhā, kayirā mālāguṇe bahū;
Just as one could make many garland strands from a heap of flowers,
Evaṃ jātena maccena, kattabbaṃ kusalaṃ bahuṃ.
So should one born mortal do much good.

54. Na pupphagandho paṭivātameti, na candanaṃ tagaramallikā;
The fragrance of flowers does not go against the wind, nor that of sandalwood, tagara, or jasmine;
Satañca gandho paṭivātameti, sabbā disā sappuriso pavāyati.
But the fragrance of the good goes against the wind; a good person pervades all directions.

55. Candanaṃ tagaraṃ vāpi, uppalaṃ atha vassikī;
Sandalwood, tagara, or even blue water lilies and jasmine—
Etesaṃ gandhajātānaṃ, sīlagandho anuttaro.
Among these kinds of perfumes, the fragrance of virtue is unsurpassed.

56. Appamatto ayaṃ gandho, yvāyaṃ tagaracandanaṃ;
Faint is this fragrance of tagara and sandalwood—
Yo ca sīlavataṃ gandho, vāti devesu uttamo.
But the fragrance of those who possess virtue blows supreme even among the gods.

57. Tesaṃ sampannasīlānaṃ, appamādavihārinaṃ;
Among those who are consummate in virtue, who dwell in diligence,
Sammadaññā vimuttānaṃ, māro maggaṃ na vindati.
And are liberated through perfect knowledge, Māra does not find the path.

58. Yathā saṅkāraṭhānasmiṃ, ujjhitasmiṃ mahāpathe;
Just as on a rubbish heap, thrown out on the highway,
Padumaṃ tattha jāyetha, sucigandhaṃ manoramaṃ.
A lotus flower might grow there, pure in scent and pleasing to the mind,

59. Evaṃ saṅkārabhūtesu, andhabhūte puthujjane;
So among the rubbish heap of blind, ordinary people,
Atirocati paññāya, sammāsambuddhasāvako.
A disciple of the Fully Awakened One shines radiantly with wisdom.
Pupphavaggo catuttho niṭṭhito.
The fourth chapter is concluded.

60. Dīghā jāgarato ratti, dīghaṃ santassa yojanaṃ;
Long is the night for the wakeful, long is a trek for the weary;
Dīgho bālānaṃ saṃsāro, saddhammaṃ avijānataṃ.
Long is saṃsāra for fools who do not understand the true Dhamma.

61. Carañce nādhigaccheyya, seyyaṃ sadisamattano;
If a traveler should not find a companion better or equal to himself,
Ekacariyaṃ daḷhaṃ kayirā, natthi bāle sahāyatā.
Then he should resolutely travel alone. With fools there is no companionship.

62. Puttā matthi dhanammatthi, iti bālo vihaññati;
The fool worries: “I have sons, I have wealth,”
Attā hi attano natthi, kuto puttā kuto dhanaṃ.
He does not even possess himself. How then sons? How then wealth?

63. Yo bālo maññati bālyaṃ, paṇḍito vāpi tena so;
A fool who considers himself foolish is, in this, like a sage.
Bālo ca paṇḍitamānī, sa ve ‘‘ bālo ’’ ti vuccati.
But a fool who is proud of his cleverness is truly called a fool.

64. Yāvajīvampi ce bālo, paṇḍitaṃ payirupāsati;
A fool who attends on a sage for a lifetime,
Na so dhammaṃ vijānāti, dabbī sūparasaṃ yathā.
Will not apprehend the Dhamma, just as a spoon can never know the soup’s flavor.

65. Muhuttamapi ce viññū, paṇḍitaṃ payirupāsati;
An intelligent person who attends on a sage even for a moment,
Khippaṃ dhammaṃ vijānāti, jivhā sūparasaṃ yathā.
Will quickly apprehend the Dhamma, like the tongue perceiving the soup’s flavor.

66. Caranti bālā dummedhā, amitteneva attanā;
Thoughtless fools wander, acting as their own enemies,
Karontā pāpakaṃ kammaṃ, yaṃ hoti kaṭukapphalaṃ.
Doing evil deeds that bear bitter fruit.

67. Na taṃ kammaṃ kataṃ sādhu, yaṃ katvā anutappati;
That deed is not well-done, which one regrets when accomplished,
Yassa assumukho rodaṃ, vipākaṃ paṭisevati.
Which results in crying and a tear-streaked face.

68. Tañca kammaṃ kataṃ sādhu, yaṃ katvā nānutappati;
That deed is well-done, which one does not regret when accomplished,
Yassa patīto sumano, vipākaṃ paṭisevati.
Which results in delight and joy.

69. Madhuvā maññati bālo, yāva pāpaṃ na paccati;
The fool thinks it honey as long as evil has not ripened;
Yadā ca paccati pāpaṃ, bālo dukkhaṃ nigacchati.
When evil ripens, the fool suffers.

70. Māse māse kusaggena, bālo bhuñjeyya bhojanaṃ;
Though month after month the fool may eat his food with the tip of the grass blade,
Na so saṅkhātadhammānaṃ, kalaṃ agghati soḷasiṃ.
He is not worth a sixteenth part of those who have realized the Dhamma.

71. Na hi pāpaṃ kataṃ kammaṃ, sajju khīraṃva muccati;
Indeed, an evil deed does not immediately curdle like fresh milk;
Ḍahantaṃ bālamanveti, bhasmacchannova pāvako.
Burning, it follows the fool, like a fire concealed by ashes.

72. Yāvadeva anatthāya, ñattaṃ bālassa jāyati;
To his own detriment knowledge arises in the fool,
Hanti bālassa sukkaṃsaṃ, muddhamassa vipātayaṃ.
It destroys his good fortune and splits his head.

73. Asantaṃ bhāvanamiccheyya, purekkhārañca bhikkhusu;
He seeks undue recognition, precedence among monks,
Āvāsesu ca issariyaṃ, pūjā parakulesu ca.
Authority in monasteries, and devotion by families not his own.

74. Mameva kata maññantu, gihīpabbajitā ubho;
“Let both householder and monk think that alone I have done this,
Mamevātivasā assu, kiccākiccesu kismici;
Let them be under my will in every task.”
Iti bālassa saṅkappo, icchā māno ca vaḍḍhati.
So thinks the fool, whose desire and pride grow.

75. Aññā hi lābhūpanisā, aññā nibbānagāminī;
There is one path to worldly gains, and a very different path to Nibbāna.
Evametaṃ abhiññāya, bhikkhu buddhassa sāvako;
Having directly known this, the bhikkhu, a disciple of the Awakened One,
Sakkāraṃ nābhinandeyya, vivekamanubrūhaye.
Should not rejoice in honor, but cultivate solitude.
Bālavaggo pañcamo niṭṭhito.
The fifth chapter is concluded.

76. Nidhīnaṃva pavattāraṃ, yaṃ passe vajjadassinaṃ;
One ought to regard another who sees one’s faults, and censures what should be censured, as a revealer of treasures.
Niggayhavādiṃ medhāviṃ, tādisaṃ paṇḍitaṃ bhaje;
One should associate with such an intelligent and wise person, a master who points out one’s faults.
Tādisaṃ bhajamānassa, seyyo hoti na pāpiyo.
For one who associates with such a person, it is for the better, not for the worse.

77. Ovadeyyānusāseyya, asabbhā ca nivāraye;
One should counsel, instruct, and restrain others from base behavior.
Satañhi so piyo hoti, asataṃ hoti appiyo.
Such a person is dear to the good, but displeasing to the bad.

78. Na bhaje pāpake mitte, na bhaje purisādhame;
Do not associate with bad friends; do not associate with depraved people.
Bhajetha mitte kalyāṇe, bhajetha purisuttame.
Associate with virtuous friends; associate with the best of people.

79. Dhammapīti sukhaṃ seti, vippasannena cetasā;
One who drinks in the Dhamma sleeps happily with a clear mind.
Ariyappavedite dhamme, sadā ramati paṇḍito.
The wise one ever-delights in the Dhamma proclaimed by the noble ones.

80. Udakañhi nayanti nettikā, usukārā namayanti tejanaṃ;
Irrigators guide water, fletchers straighten the shaft.
Dāruṃ namayanti tacchakā, attānaṃ damayanti paṇḍitā.
Carpenters bend wood; the wise master themselves.

81. Selo yathā ekaghano, vātena na samīrati;
Just as a solid rock is unmoved by the wind,
Evaṃ nindāpasaṃsāsu, na samiñjanti paṇḍitā.
So the wise are unmoved by blame or praise.

82. Yathāpi rahado gambhīro, vippasanno anāvilo;
Just as a deep lake is clear and undisturbed,
Evaṃ dhammāni sutvāna, vippasīdanti paṇḍitā.
So the wise become clear, having heard the teachings.

83. Sabbattha ve sappurisā cajanti, na kāmakāmā lapayanti santo;
The good are indeed detached in every way; the peaceful do not chatter, craving pleasures.
Sukhena phuṭṭhā atha vā dukhena, na uccāvacaṃ paṇḍitā dassayanti.
Though touched by comfort or affliction, the wise manifest no elation or depression.

84. Na attahetu na parassa hetu, na puttamicche na dhanaṃ na raṭṭhaṃ;
One who would not want, for the sake of oneself or another, a son, wealth, or a kingdom;
Na iccheyya adhammena samiddhimattano, sa sīlavā paññavā dhammiko siyā.
And who would not wish for success through unjust means—such a person is virtuous, wise, and righteous.

85. Appakā te manussesu, ye janā pāragāmino;
Few are those who have gone to the other shore,
Athāyaṃ itarā pajā, tīramevānudhāvati.
While the rest of humanity only runs about on this side.

86. Ye ca kho sammadakkhāte, dhamme dhammānuvattino;
But those who live according to the well-taught Dhamma,
Te janā pāramessanti, maccudheyyaṃ suduttaraṃ.
Those people will go beyond the realm of death, so difficult to cross.

87. Kaṇhaṃ dhammaṃ vippahāya, sukkaṃ bhāvetha paṇḍito;
Having abandoned the dark state, the wise one should cultivate the light;
Okā anokamāgamma, viveke yattha dūramaṃ.
Having come from home to homelessness, to that solitude so difficult to enjoy.

88. Tatrābhiratimiccheyya, hitvā kāme akiñcano;
There, one should wish for delight, having abandoned sense pleasures, a person of nothing.
Pariyodapeyya attānaṃ, cittaklesehi paṇḍito.
The wise one should cleanse himself from the defilements of the mind.

89. Yesaṃ sambodhiyaṅgesu, sammā cittaṃ subhāvitaṃ;
Those whose minds are well-developed in the factors of awakening,
Ādānapaṭinissagge, anupādāya ye ratā;
Who, without clinging, delight in the relinquishing of grasping,
Khīṇāsavā jutimanto, te loke parinibbutā.
Those with taints destroyed, the bright ones, are completely emancipated in this world.
Paṇḍitavaggo chaṭṭho niṭṭhito.
The sixth chapter, on the Wise, is concluded.

90. Gataddhino visokassa, vippamuttassa sabbadhi;
90. For one who has completed the journey, who is without sorrow,
Sabbaganthappahīnassa, pariḷāho na vijjati.
Who is freed in every way, who has abandoned all fetters, burning heat does not exist.

91. Uyyuñjanti satīmanto, na nikete ramanti te;
91. Those who are mindful depart; they do not delight in a house.
Haṃsāva pallalaṃ hitvā, okamokaṃ jahanti te.
Like geese who abandon a pond, they abandon every home.

92. Yesaṃ sannicayo natthi, ye pariññātabhojanā;
92. Those who have no accumulation, who know well the role of food,
Suññato animitto ca, vimokkho yesaṃ gocaro;
Whose field is empty, unmarked freedom, is their destination;
Ākāse va sakuntānaṃ, gati tesaṃ durannayā.
Like birds in the sky, their travel is difficult to discern.

93. Yassāsavā parikkhīṇā, āhāre ca anissito;
93. One whose toxins are completely ended, who is independent regarding food,
Suññato animitto ca, vimokkho yassa gocaro;
Whose field is empty, unmarked freedom, is their destination;
Ākāse va sakuntānaṃ, padaṃ tassa durannayaṃ.
Like birds in the sky, their state is difficult to discern.

94. Yassindriyāni samathaṅgatāni, assā yathā sārathinā sudantā;
94. One whose faculties have reached peace, like horses well-tamed by a trainer,
Pahīnamānassa anāsavassa, devāpi tassa pihayanti tādino.
Who has abandoned pride and is free from toxins, that Stable One is envied even by the gods.

95. Pathavisamo no virujjhati, indakhilupamo tādi subbato;
95. Like the earth, he does not oppose; the Stable One is like a pillar of Indra, a person of good manners;
Rahadova apetakaddamo, saṃsārā na bhavanti tādino.
Like a pond without mud, for that Stable One, wandering does not occur.

96. Santaṃ tassa manaṃ hoti, santā vācā ca kamma ca;
96. Calm is the mind of such a one, calm is the speech and action,
Sammadaññā vimuttassa, upasantassa tādino.
Of one completely liberated through final knowledge, the Calm One, the Stable One.

97. Assaddho akataññū ca, sandhicchedo ca yo naro;
97. The person who is without faith, ungrateful, a breaker of connections,
Hatāvakāso vantāso, sa ve uttamaporiso.
Who has destroyed opportunities and abandoned desire: he is indeed the ultimate person.

98. Gāme vā yadi vāraññe, ninne vā yadi vā thale;
98. Whether in the village or in the forest, in the valley or on high ground,
Yattha arahanto viharanti, taṃ bhūmirāmaṇeyyakaṃ.
Wherever the arahants dwell, that is a beautiful land.

99. Ramaṇīyāni araññāni, yattha na ramatī jano;
99. Delightful are the forests where few find delight;
Vītarāgā ramissanti, na te kāmagavesino.
Those without lust will delight there, for they are not seekers of sensual pleasure.
Arahantavaggo sattamo niṭṭhito.
The seventh chapter on the Arahants is completed.

100. Sahassamapi ce vācā, anatthapadasaṃhitā;
Better than a thousand vacuous statements,
Ekaṃ atthapadaṃ seyyo, yaṃ sutvā upasammati.
Is one meaningful word, which, having been heard, brings peace.

101. Sahassamapi ce gāthā, anatthapadasaṃhitā;
Better than a thousand vacuous verses,
Ekaṃ gāthāpadaṃ seyyo, yaṃ sutvā upasammati.
Is one line of verse, which, having been heard, brings peace.

102. Yo ca gāthā sataṃ bhāse, anatthapadasaṃhitā;
Better than a recitation of one hundred meaningless verses,
Ekaṃ dhammapadaṃ seyyo, yaṃ sutvā upasammati.
Is one Dhamma word, which, having been heard, brings peace.

103. Yo sahassaṃ sahassena, saṅgāme mānuse jine;
Greater in battle than one who conquers thousands upon thousands,
Ekañca jeyyamattānaṃ, sa ve saṅgāmajuttamo.
Is the one who conquers himself; he is the greatest in battle.

104. Attā have jitaṃ seyyo, yā cāyaṃ itarā pajā;
It is better to conquer oneself than others;
Attadantassa posassa, niccaṃ saññatacārino.
For the self-tamed one, ever-vigilant in self-control,

105. Neva devo na gandhabbo, na māro saha brahmunā;
Neither a god nor a divine musician, nor Māra and Brahmā together,
Jitaṃ apajitaṃ kayirā, tathārūpassa jantuno.
Could turn such a victory into defeat for such a person.

106. Māse māse sahassena, yo yajetha sataṃ samaṃ;
Better than offering a thousand sacrifices month after month for a hundred years,
Ekañca bhāvitattānaṃ, muhuttamapi pūjaye;
Is one moment’s homage to one who has developed himself;
Sāyeva pūjanā seyyo, yañce vassasataṃ hutaṃ.
That homage alone is better than a hundred years of sacrifice.

107. Yo ca vassasataṃ jantu, aggiṃ paricare vane;
Better than living one hundred years in the forest tending the sacred fire,
Ekañca bhāvitattānaṃ, muhuttamapi pūjaye;
Is one moment’s homage to one who has developed himself;
Sāyeva pūjanā seyyo, yañce vassasataṃ hutaṃ.
That homage alone is better than a hundred years of sacrifice.

108. Yaṃ kiñci yiṭṭhaṃ va hutaṃ va loke, saṃvaccharaṃ yajetha puññapekkho;
Whatever offering or sacrifice in the world a merit-seeker might offer for a year,
Sabbampi taṃ na catubhāgameti, abhivādanā ujjugatesu seyyo.
All this is not one-fourth the merit gained from showing respect to the upright.

109. Abhivādanasīlissa, niccaṃ vuḍḍhāpacāyino;
For one who is respectful, who constantly reveres the elders,
Cattāro dhammā vaḍḍhanti, āyu vaṇṇo sukhaṃ balaṃ.
Four things grow: life, beauty, happiness, and strength.

110. Yo ca vassasataṃ jīve, dussīlo asamāhito;
Better than living one hundred years, immoral and ungrounded,
Ekāhaṃ jīvitaṃ seyyo, sīlavantassa jhāyino.
Is living one day, virtuous and absorbed in meditation.

111. Yo ca vassasataṃ jīve, duppañño asamāhito;
Better than living one hundred years, foolish and ungrounded,
Ekāhaṃ jīvitaṃ seyyo, paññavantassa jhāyino.
Is living one day, wise and absorbed in meditation.

112. Yo ca vassasataṃ jīve, kusīto hīnavīriyo;
Better than living one hundred years, indolent and slothful,
Ekāhaṃ jīvitaṃ seyyo, vīriyamārabhato daḷhaṃ.
Is living one day with steadfast vigor.

113. Yo ca vassasataṃ jīve, apassaṃ udayabbayaṃ;
Better than living one hundred years without seeing arising and vanishing,
Ekāhaṃ jīvitaṃ seyyo, passato udayabbayaṃ.
Is living one day, seeing arising and vanishing.

114. Yo ca vassasataṃ jīve, apassaṃ amataṃ padaṃ;
Better than living one hundred years without seeing the deathless state,
Ekāhaṃ jīvitaṃ seyyo, passato amataṃ padaṃ.
Is living one day, seeing the deathless state.

115. Yo ca vassasataṃ jīve, apassaṃ dhammamuttamaṃ;
Better than living one hundred years without seeing the supreme Dhamma,
Ekāhaṃ jīvitaṃ seyyo, passato dhammamuttamaṃ.
Is living one day, seeing the supreme Dhamma.
Sahassavaggo aṭṭhamo niṭṭhito.
The eighth chapter on the Thousands is concluded.

116. Abhittharetha kalyāṇe, pāpā cittaṃ nivāraye;
Hasten to do good; restrain the mind from evil.
Dandhañhi karoto puññaṃ, pāpasmiṃ ramatī mano.
For the mind of one who is slow in doing good delights in evil.

117. Pāpañce puriso kayirā, na naṃ kayirā punappunaṃ;
If a man should do evil, he should not do it again and again;
Na tamhi chandaṃ kayirātha, dukkho pāpassa uccayo.
he should not cultivate desire for it; accumulated evil brings suffering.

118. Puññañce puriso kayirā, kayirā naṃ punappunaṃ;
If a man should do good, he should do it again and again;
Tamhi chandaṃ kayirātha, sukho puññassa uccayo.
he should cultivate desire for it; accumulated good brings happiness.

119. Pāpopi passati bhadraṃ, yāva pāpaṃ na paccati;
Even an evildoer sees good fortune as long as the evil has not ripened;
Yadā ca paccati pāpaṃ, atha pāpo pāpāni passati.
but when the evil does ripen, then the evildoer sees evil.

120. Bhadropi passati pāpaṃ, yāva bhadraṃ na paccati;
Even a good person sees evil as long as the good has not ripened;
Yadā ca paccati bhadraṃ, atha bhadro bhadrāni passati.
but when the good does ripen, then the good person sees good fortune.

121. Māvamaññetha pāpassa, na mantaṃ āgamissati;
Do not disregard evil, thinking, 'It will not come to me';
Udabindunipātena, udakumbhopi pūrati;
by the falling of drops of water, a water-pot is filled;
Bālo pūrati pāpassa, thokaṃ thokampi ācinaṃ.
the fool becomes full of evil, accumulating it little by little.

122. Māvamaññetha puññassa, na mantaṃ āgamissati;
Do not disregard good, thinking, 'It will not come to me';
Udabindunipātena, udakumbhopi pūrati;
by the falling of drops of water, a water-pot is filled;
Dhīro pūrati puññassa, thokaṃ thokampi ācinaṃ.
the wise one becomes full of good, accumulating it little by little.

123. Vāṇijova bhayaṃ maggaṃ, appasattho mahaddhano;
Just as a merchant with much wealth and a small caravan avoids a dangerous road,
Visaṃ jīvitukāmova, pāpāni parivajjaye.
and just as one who desires life avoids poison, one should avoid evil deeds.

124. Pāṇimhi ce vaṇo nāssa, hareyya pāṇinā visaṃ;
If there is no wound in the hand, one could carry poison in the hand;
Nābbaṇaṃ visamanveti, natthi pāpaṃ akubbato.
poison does not affect one without a wound; there is no evil for one who does not do it.

125. Yo appaduṭṭhassa narassa dussati, suddhassa posassa anaṅgaṇassa;
Whoever offends an innocent person, who is pure and without defilement,
Tameva bālaṃ pacceti pāpaṃ, sukhumo rajo paṭivātaṃva khitto.
that evil returns to that very fool, like fine dust thrown against the wind.

126. Gabbhameke uppajjanti, nirayaṃ pāpakammino;
Some are reborn in a womb; evildoers are reborn in hell;
Saggaṃ sugatino yanti, parinibbanti anāsavā.
the righteous go to heaven; those without cankers attain final liberation.

127. Na antalikkhe na samuddamajjhe, na pabbatānaṃ vivaraṃ pavissa;
Not in the sky, not in the middle of the ocean, not by entering into a mountain cave,
Na vijjatī so jagatippadeso, yatthaṭṭhito mucceyya pāpakammā.
is there a place on earth where, having stood, one would be freed from an evil deed.

128. Na antalikkhe na samuddamajjhe, na pabbatānaṃ vivaraṃ pavissa;
Not in the sky, not in the middle of the ocean, not by entering into a mountain cave,
Na vijjatī so jagatippadeso, yatthaṭṭhitaṃ nappasaheyya maccu.
is there a place on earth where, having stood, death would not overcome one.
Pāpavaggo navamo niṭṭhito.
The ninth chapter on Evil is concluded.

129. Sabbe tasanti daṇḍassa, sabbe bhāyanti maccuno;
All tremble at a thrashing. All fear dying.
Attānaṃ upamaṃ katvā, na haneyya na ghātaye.
Likening others to oneself, one should not kill or lead another to kill.

130. Sabbe tasanti daṇḍassa, sabbesaṃ jīvitaṃ piyaṃ;
All tremble at a thrashing. All hold life dear.
Attānaṃ upamaṃ katvā, na haneyya na ghātaye.
Likening others to oneself, one should not kill or lead another to kill.

131. Sukhakāmāni bhūtāni, yo daṇḍena vihiṃsati;
Whoever does violence with a stick to sentient beings who desire happiness,
Attano sukhamesāno, pecca so na labhate sukhaṃ.
While seeking happiness for himself, will realize no happiness after death.

132. Sukhakāmāni bhūtāni, yo daṇḍena na hiṃsati;
Whoever does not do violence with a stick to sentient beings who desire happiness,
Attano sukhamesāno, pecca so labhate sukhaṃ.
While seeking happiness for himself, will realize happiness after death.

133. Māvoca pharusaṃ kañci, vuttā paṭivadeyyu taṃ;
Do not speak harshly to anyone; those spoken to would respond in kind.
Dukkhā hi sārambhakathā, paṭidaṇḍā phuseyyu taṃ.
Contentious speech is painful; retaliation would befall you.

134. Sace neresi attānaṃ, kaṃso upahato yathā;
If you are unstirred, like a broken gong,
Esa pattosi nibbānaṃ, sārambho te na vijjati.
You have attained Nibbāna; no contention exists within you.

135. Yathā daṇḍena gopālo, gāvo pājeti gocaraṃ;
Just as a cowherd drives his cows to pasture with a stick,
Evaṃ jarā ca maccu ca, āyuṃ pājenti pāṇinaṃ.
So old age and death drive the life of sentient beings.

136. Atha pāpāni kammāni, karaṃ bālo na bujjhati;
When a fool performs evil deeds, he does not recognize them.
Sehi kammehi dummedho, aggidaḍḍhova tappati.
Lacking discerning wisdom, he is tormented by his own actions, as if burned by fire.

137. Yo daṇḍena adaṇḍesu, appaduṭṭhesu dussati;
Whoever does violence to the innocent and peaceable,
Dasannamaññataraṃ ṭhānaṃ, khippameva nigacchati.
Will quickly encounter one of ten states:

138. Vedanaṃ pharusaṃ jāniṃ, sarīrassa ca bhedanaṃ;
Intense pain, a fine, bodily injury,
Garukaṃ vāpi ābādhaṃ, cittakkhepañca pāpuṇe.
Or serious disease, or mental derangement,

139. Rājato vā upasaggaṃ, abbhakkhānañca dāruṇaṃ;
Oppression from kings, or to misrepresent, or cruel slander,
Parikkhayañca ñātīnaṃ, bhogānañca pabhaṅguraṃ.
Loss of relatives, or the depletion of his possessions.

140. Atha vāssa agārāni, aggi ḍahati pāvako;
Or a fire burns down his houses,
Kāyassa bhedā duppañño, nirayaṃ sopapajjati.
And upon the breaking up of the body, the person of weak discernment reappears in hell.

141. Na naggacariyā na jaṭā na paṅkā, nānāsakā thaṇḍilasāyikā vā;
Neither nakedness, nor matted hair, nor mud, nor fasting, nor sleeping on the ground,
Rajojallaṃ ukkuṭikappadhānaṃ, sodhenti maccaṃ avitiṇṇakaṅkhaṃ.
Nor dust and dirt, nor squatting, can purify a mortal who has not overcome doubt.

142. Alaṅkato cepi samaṃ careyya, santo danto niyato brahmacārī;
Even if one is luxuriously attired, if one is peaceful, tamed, assured, and celibate,
Sabbesu bhūtesu nidhāya daṇḍaṃ, so brāhmaṇo so samaṇo sa bhikkhu.
Having laid down the stick toward any sentient being, such is a brahmin, a recluse, a bhikkhu.

143. Hirīnisedho puriso, koci lokasmi vijjati;
Where in this world exists a man who is restrained by conscience and avoids blame,
Yo niddaṃ apabodheti, asso bhadro kasāmiva.
Who awakens from sleep, like a thoroughbred horse the whip?

144. Asso yathā bhadro kasāniviṭṭho, ātāpino saṃvegino bhavātha;
Like a thoroughbred horse touched by the whip, be ardent and alarmed.
Saddhāya sīlena ca vīriyena ca, samādhinā dhammavinicchayena ca;
Through faith, virtuous behavior, and vigor, through concentration and investigation of truth,
Sampannavijjācaraṇā patissatā, jahissatha dukkhamidaṃ anappakaṃ.
Possessed of right knowledge and conduct, and mindful, you will leave this abundant suffering behind.

145. Udakañhi nayanti nettikā, usukārā namayanti tejanaṃ;
Irrigators guide water; fletchers straighten shafts;
Dāruṃ namayanti tacchakā, attānaṃ damayanti subbatā.
Carpenters bend wood; the well-practiced master themselves.
Daṇḍavaggo dasamo niṭṭhito.
The tenth chapter on Punishment is completed.

146. Ko nu hāso kimānando, niccaṃ pajjalite sati;
Why laughter, why joy, when it is always aflame?
Andhakārena onaddhā, padīpaṃ na gavesatha.
Covered in darkness, do you not seek the light?

147. Passa cittakataṃ bimbaṃ, arukāyaṃ samussitaṃ;
Look at this beautified image, a diseased mass of sores, raised up,
Āturaṃ bahusaṅkappaṃ, yassa natthi dhuvaṃ ṭhiti.
Diseased, with many plans, which has no permanence.

148. Parijiṇṇamidaṃ rūpaṃ, roganīḷaṃ pabhaṅguraṃ;
This body is wasted away, a nest of illness, fragile;
Bhijjati pūtisandeho, maraṇantañhi jīvitaṃ.
This foul mass breaks up, for truly life ends in death.

149. Yānimāni apatthāni, alābūneva sārade;
On seeing these gray bones, scattered like gourds in autumn—
Kāpotakāni aṭṭhīni, tāni disvāna kā rati.
What is the fondness?

150. Aṭṭhīnaṃ nagaraṃ kataṃ, maṃsalohitalepanaṃ;
It is a city made of bones, plastered in flesh and blood,
Yattha jarā ca maccu ca, māno makkho ca ohito.
Where old age, death, conceit, and contempt are stored.

151. Jīranti ve rājarathā sucittā, atho sarīrampi jaraṃ upeti;
The king’s chariots, so splendidly adorned, decay, and the body too decays;
Satañca dhammo na jaraṃ upeti, santo have sabbhi pavedayanti.
But the Dhamma of the good does not decay; indeed, the good declare it to one another.

152. Appassutāyaṃ puriso, balibaddhova jīrati;
He of little learning grows old like an ox;
Maṃsāni tassa vaḍḍhanti, paññā tassa na vaḍḍhati.
His bulk increases, but his wisdom does not.

153. Anekajātisaṃsāraṃ, sandhāvissaṃ anibbisaṃ;
Through many rounds of birth have I run, seeking but not finding,
Gahakāraṃ gavesanto, dukkhā jāti punappunaṃ.
The builder of this house; painful is birth, again and again.

154. Gahakāraka diṭṭhosi, puna gehaṃ na kāhasi;
House-builder, you are seen! You will build no more;
Sabbā te phāsukā bhaggā, gahakūṭaṃ visaṅkhataṃ;
Your rafters are broken, the roof destroyed;
Visaṅkhāragataṃ cittaṃ, taṇhānaṃ khayamajjhagā.
The mind, having gone to the Unconditioned, has attained the destruction of craving.

155. Acaritvā brahmacariyaṃ, aladdhā yobbane dhanaṃ;
Not having lived a spiritual life, not having obtained wealth in youth,
Jiṇṇakoñcāva jhāyanti, khīṇamaccheva pallale.
They brood like old herons in a lake without fish.

156. Acaritvā brahmacariyaṃ, aladdhā yobbane dhanaṃ;
Not having lived a spiritual life, not having obtained wealth in youth,
Senti cāpātikhīṇāva, purāṇāni anutthunaṃ.
They lie like arrows shot from a bow, lamenting the past.
Jarāvaggo ekādasamo niṭṭhito.
The eleventh chapter on Old Age is concluded.

157. Attānañce piyaṃ jaññā, rakkheyya naṃ surakkhitaṃ;
If one would know oneself as dear, one would keep oneself well-protected;
Tiṇṇaṃ aññataraṃ yāmaṃ, paṭijaggeyya paṇḍito.
The learned person should keep watch throughout the three watches of the night.

158. Attānameva paṭhamaṃ, patirūpe nivesaye;
One should first establish oneself in what is proper;
Athaññamanusāseyya, na kilisseyya paṇḍito.
Then only could the learned person instruct others and remain unstained.

159. Attānaṃ ce tathā kayirā, yathāññamanusāsati;
If one would only do oneself what one instructs others to do,
Sudanto vata dametha, attā hi kira duddamo.
Then, well-restrained, he would well-train others; truly, training oneself is most difficult.

160. Attā hi attano nātho, ko hi nātho paro siyā;
Truly oneself is one’s own protector. What other protector could there be?
Attanā hi sudantena, nāthaṃ labhati dullabhaṃ.
Only by being well-trained oneself does one obtain the true protector, so hard to gain.

161. Attanā hi kataṃ pāpaṃ, attajaṃ attasambhavaṃ;
By oneself is evil done; it proceeds from oneself, it originates from oneself.
Abhimatthati dummedhaṃ, vajiraṃ vasmamayaṃ maṇiṃ.
It crushes the fool, like a diamond crushes a precious stone.

162. Yassa accantadussīlyaṃ, māluvā sālamivotthataṃ;
He whose depraved conduct is extreme, like a creeper over a sala tree,
Karoti so tathattānaṃ, yathā naṃ icchatī diso.
Does to himself what an enemy desires for him.

163. Sukarāni asādhūni, attano ahitāni ca;
It is easy to do wrong, so harmful to oneself;
Yaṃ ve hitañca sādhuñca, taṃ ve paramadukkaraṃ.
It is indeed most difficult to do what is beneficial and good.

164. Yo sāsanaṃ arahataṃ, ariyānaṃ dhammajīvinaṃ;
He who scorns the teaching of the Arahants,
Paṭikkosati dummedho, diṭṭhiṃ nissāya pāpikaṃ;
Those noble ones who live the Dhamma, is the fool who relies on evil views;
Phalāni kaṭṭhakasseva, attaghātāya phallati.
Like the fruit of a bamboo, he ripens to self-destruction.

165. Attanā hi kataṃ pāpaṃ, attanā saṃkilissati;
By oneself alone is evil done, by oneself one becomes defiled;
Attanā akataṃ pāpaṃ, attanāva visujjhati;
Evil is undone by oneself, by oneself alone can one become cleansed.
Suddhī asuddhi paccattaṃ, nāñño aññaṃ visodhaye.
Purity and impurity arise from oneself; no one can purify another.

166. Attadatthaṃ paratthena, bahunāpi na hāpaye;
Neglect not your own welfare for another’s, however great.
Attadatthamabhiññāya, sadatthapasuto siyā.
If you fully understood your own welfare, you would pursue the true attainment.
Attavaggo dvādasamo niṭṭhito.
The twelfth chapter is concluded.

167. Hīnaṃ dhammaṃ na seveyya, pamādena na saṃvase;
Do not follow an inferior teaching; do not live in negligence;
Micchādiṭṭhiṃ na seveyya, na siyā lokavaḍḍhano.
Do not embrace wrong views; do not be a world-enhancer.

168. Uttiṭṭhe nappamajjeyya, dhammaṃ sucaritaṃ care;
Arise and do not be negligent; practice the Dhamma well;
Dhammacārī sukhaṃ seti, asmiṃ loke paramhi ca.
One who dwells in the Dhamma sleeps happily, in this world and the next.

169. Dhammaṃ care sucaritaṃ, na naṃ duccaritaṃ care;
Practice the Dhamma well, not poorly;
Dhammacārī sukhaṃ seti, asmiṃ loke paramhi ca.
One who dwells in the Dhamma sleeps happily, in this world and the next.

170. Yathā pubbuḷakaṃ passe, yathā passe marīcikaṃ;
Look upon the world as a bubble; look upon the world as a mirage;
Evaṃ lokaṃ avekkhantaṃ, maccurājā na passati.
The King of Death does not see one who looks upon the world thus.

171. Etha passathimaṃ lokaṃ, cittaṃ rājarathūpamaṃ;
Come, look at this world as an adorned royal chariot;
Yattha bālā visīdanti, natthi saṅgo vijānataṃ.
Where fools sink down, the discriminating have no attachment.

172. Yo ca pubbe pamajjitvā, pacchā so nappamajjati;
The one who was previously negligent, but later does not grow negligent;
Somaṃ lokaṃ pabhāseti, abbhā muttova candimā.
Illumines this world, like the moon released from clouds.

173. Yassa pāpaṃ kataṃ kammaṃ, kusalena pidhīyati;
The one whose evil action is covered by wholesomeness;
Somaṃ lokaṃ pabhāseti, abbhā muttova candimā.
Illumines this world, like the moon released from clouds.

174. Andhabhūto ayaṃ loko, tanukettha vipassati;
Blind is this world; few see clearly here;
Sakuṇo jālamuttova, appo saggāya gacchati.
Like a bird released from a snare, few go to heaven.

175. Haṃsādiccapathe yanti, ākāse yanti iddhiyā;
Geese travel by the path of the sun; they go through the sky by marvelous power;
Nīyanti dhīrā lokamhā, jetvā māraṃ savāhiniṃ.
The wise are led from the world, having vanquished Māra and his mount.

176. Ekaṃ dhammaṃ atītassa, musāvādissa jantuno;
Of the one who has transgressed the one Dhamma, the person given to false speech;
Vitiṇṇaparalokassa, natthi pāpaṃ akāriyaṃ.
Who has rejected the next world, there is no evil that he would not do.

177. Na ve kadariyā devalokaṃ vajanti, bālā have nappasaṃsanti dānaṃ;
Truly, the stingy do not go to the world of the gods; the foolish do not praise a gift;
Dhīro ca dānaṃ anumodamāno, teneva so hoti sukhī parattha.
But the wise one, rejoicing in a gift, is thereby happy in the next world.

178. Pathabyā ekarajjena, saggassa gamanena vā;
Sole sovereignty over the earth, or going to heaven,
Sabbalokādhipaccena, sotāpattiphalaṃ varaṃ.
Or lordship over all worlds; the fruit of stream-entry is supreme.
Lokavaggo terasamo niṭṭhito.
The thirteenth chapter on the world is concluded.

179. Yassa jitaṃ nāvajīyati, jitaṃ yassa no yāti koci loke;
He whose victory cannot be undone, a conquest no one in the world can pursue;
Taṃ buddhamanantagocaraṃ, apadaṃ kena padena nessatha.
This is the Buddha, trackless in an endless field. By what path would you guide him?

180. Yassa jālinī visattikā, taṇhā natthi kuhiñci netave;
The one who has no snare, no craving, no thirst to carry him anywhere;
Taṃ buddhamanantagocaraṃ, apadaṃ kena padena nessatha.
This is the Buddha, trackless in an endless field. By what path would you guide him?

181. Ye jhānapasutā dhīrā, nekkhammūpasame ratā;
The wise, intent on meditation, delighting in renunciation and calm,
Devāpi tesaṃ pihayanti, sambuddhānaṃ satīmataṃ.
Even the gods envy them, the mindful and fully awakened ones.

182. Kiccho manussapaṭilābho, kicchaṃ maccāna jīvitaṃ;
Difficult is the obtaining of the human state, difficult is the life of mortals;
Kicchaṃ saddhammassavanaṃ, kiccho buddhānamuppādo.
Difficult is the hearing of the good Dhamma, difficult is the arising of Buddhas.

183. Sabbapāpassa akaraṇaṃ, kusalassa upasampadā;
Doing no evil, undertaking the good,
Sacittapariyodapanaṃ, etaṃ buddhāna sāsanaṃ.
Purifying one’s mind: this is the teaching of the Buddhas.

184. Khantī paramaṃ tapo titikkhā, nibbānaṃ paramaṃ vadanti buddhā;
Forbearance is the highest asceticism; the Buddhas teach that Nibbāna is supreme.
Na hi pabbajito parūpaghātī, na samaṇo hoti paraṃ viheṭhayanto.
One who hurts another is no renunciant; one who harms another is no recluse.

185. Anūpavādo anūpaghāto, pātimokkhe ca saṃvaro;
Not insulting, not harming, restraint in the Pātimokkha,
Mattaññutā ca bhattasmiṃ, pantañca sayanāsanaṃ;
Moderation in food, dwelling in solitude, bed-and-chair in a remote place,
Adhicitte ca āyogo, etaṃ buddhāna sāsanaṃ.
Devotion to higher mind: this is the teaching of the Buddhas.

186. Na kahāpaṇavassena, titti kāmesu vijjati;
Not even by a rain of coins is there satisfaction in sensual pleasures.
Appassādā dukhā kāmā, iti viññāya paṇḍito.
The learned one knows that sensual pleasures are of little taste and cause pain.

187. Api dibbesu kāmesu, ratiṃ so nādhigacchati;
He does not find delight even in heavenly sensual pleasures;
Taṇhakkhayarato hoti, sammāsambuddhasāvako.
The disciple of the Fully Awakened One is devoted to craving’s destruction.

188. Bahuṃ ve saraṇaṃ yanti, pabbatāni vanāni ca;
People driven by fear seek many refuges, mountains, forests,
Ārāmarukkhacetyāni, manussā bhayatajjitā.
Shrine parks and trees.

189. Netaṃ kho saraṇaṃ khemaṃ, netaṃ saraṇamuttamaṃ;
Truly, that refuge is not safe, that refuge is not supreme.
Netaṃ saraṇamāgamma, sabbadukkhā pamuccati.
Having come to that refuge, one is not freed from all suffering.

190. Yo ca buddhañca dhammañca, saṅghañca saraṇaṃ gato;
But one who has gone for refuge to the Buddha, the Dhamma, and the Sangha,
Cattāri ariyasaccāni, sammappaññāya passati.
Sees, with right wisdom, the four Noble Truths:

191. Dukkhaṃ dukkhasamuppādaṃ, dukkhassa ca atikkamaṃ;
Suffering, the origin of suffering, the overcoming of suffering,
Ariyaṃ caṭṭhaṅgikaṃ maggaṃ, dukkhūpasamagāminaṃ.
And the Noble Eightfold Path leading to the cessation of suffering.

192. Etaṃ kho saraṇaṃ khemaṃ, etaṃ saraṇamuttamaṃ;
Truly, this refuge is safe, this refuge is supreme.
Etaṃ saraṇamāgamma, sabbadukkhā pamuccati.
Having come to this refuge, one is truly freed from all suffering.

193. Dullabho purisājañño, na so sabbattha jāyati;
A remarkable man is rare to find; he is not born everywhere.
Yattha so jāyati dhīro, taṃ kulaṃ sukhamedhati.
Where such a wise one is born, that family happily flourishes.

194. Sukho buddhānamuppādo, sukhā saddhammadesanā;
Happy is the arising of Buddhas, happy is the teaching of the true Dhamma,
Sukhā saṅghassa sāmaggī, samaggānaṃ tapo sukho.
Happy is the harmony of the Sangha, happy is the austere practice of those in harmony.

195. Pūjārahe pūjayato, buddhe yadi va sāvake;
If one venerates those worthy of veneration, Buddhas or their disciples,
Papañcasamatikkante, tiṇṇasokapariddave.
Who have transcended proliferations and passed beyond grief and sorrow,

196. Te tādise pūjayato, nibbute akutobhaye;
If one venerates such beings, who have reached Nibbāna and fear nothing,
Na sakkā puññaṃ saṅkhātuṃ, imettamapi kenaci.
The merit cannot be measured by anyone as 'this much'.
Buddhavaggo cuddasamo niṭṭhito.
The fourteenth chapter on the Buddha is completed.

197. Susukhaṃ vata jīvāma, verinesu averino;
197. So very happily we live, without hostility among the hostile;
Verinesu manussesu, viharāma averino.
Among the hostile, we dwell without hostility.

198. Susukhaṃ vata jīvāma, āturesu anāturā;
198. So very happily we live, healthy among the ill;
Āturesu manussesu, viharāma anāturā.
Among the ill, we dwell in health.

199. Susukhaṃ vata jīvāma, ussukesu anussukā;
199. So very happily we live, without longing among those with longing;
Ussukesu manassesu, viharāma anussukā.
Among those with longing, we dwell without longing.

200. Susukhaṃ vata jīvāma, yesaṃ no natthi kiñcanaṃ;
200. So very happily we live, we who have nothing;
Pītibhakkhā bhavissāma, devā ābhassarā yathā.
We will feed on rapture, like the gods of streaming radiance.

201. Jayaṃ veraṃ pasavati, dukkhaṃ seti parājito;
201. Conquest gives birth to enmity; the defeated lie in suffering.
Upasanto sukhaṃ seti, hitvā jayaparājayaṃ.
The calm one rests at ease, having abandoned conquest and defeat.

202. Natthi rāgasamo aggi, natthi dosasamo kali;
202. There is no fire like passion; there is no fault like ill will;
Natthi khandhasamā dukkhā, natthi santiparaṃ sukhaṃ.
There is no suffering like the aggregates; there is no happiness higher than peace.

203. Jighacchāparamā rogā, saṅkhāraparamā dukhā;
203. Hunger is the greatest illness; conditioned things are the greatest suffering;
Etaṃ ñatvā yathābhūtaṃ, nibbānaṃ paramaṃ sukhaṃ.
Having known this as it really is, Nibbāna is the supreme happiness.

204. Ārogyaparamā lābhā, santuṭṭhiparamaṃ dhanaṃ;
204. Health is the finest possession; contentment is the greatest money;
Vissāsaparamā ñāti, nibbānaṃ paramaṃ sukhaṃ.
Trust is the best of relatives; Nibbāna is the supreme happiness.

205. Pavivekarasaṃ pitvā, rasaṃ upasamassa ca;
205. Having drunk the nectar of solitude and the nectar of tranquility,
Niddaro hoti nippāpo, dhammapītirasaṃ pivaṃ.
One becomes free of fear and free of evil, drinking the nectar of Dhamma delight.

206. Sāhu dassanamariyānaṃ, sannivāso sadā sukho;
206. Seeing the noble ones is good; their company is always pleasant.
Adassanena bālānaṃ, niccameva sukhī siyā.
By not seeing fools, one would be happy always.

207. Bālasaṅgatacārī hi, dīghamaddhāna socati;
207. For one who walks with fools grieves a long time;
Dukkho bālehi saṃvāso, amitteneva sabbadā;
Living with fools is always painful, like living with an enemy;
Dhīro ca sukhasaṃvāso, ñātīnaṃva samāgamo.
But the wise one is pleasant to live with, like the assembly of relatives.

208. Tasmā hi –
208. Therefore:
Dhīrañca paññañca bahussutañca, dhorayhasīlaṃ vatavantamariyaṃ;
Follow the wise one, the intelligent, the learned, the one committed to virtue, the noble one, the one bearing the burden,
Taṃ tādisaṃ sappurisaṃ sumedhaṃ, bhajetha nakkhattapathaṃva candimā.
Follow that noble person of great wisdom, as the moon follows the path of the stars.
Sukhavaggo pannarasamo niṭṭhito.
The fifteenth chapter on happiness is concluded.

209. Ayoge yuñjamattānaṃ, yogasmiñca ayojayaṃ;
Devoting oneself to what is not to be done, and not doing what should be done;
Atthaṃ hitvā piyaggāhī, pihetattānuyoginaṃ.
Having abandoned what is worthwhile, one who clings to what is dear envies those who apply themselves.

210. Mā piyehi samāgañchi, appiyehi kudācanaṃ;
Never associate with those who are dear, nor with those who are abrasive;
Piyānaṃ adassanaṃ dukkhaṃ, appiyānañca dassanaṃ.
Not seeing those who are dear is suffering, and seeing those who are abrasive is also suffering.

211. Tasmā piyaṃ na kayirātha, piyāpāyo hi pāpako;
Therefore, one should not hold anything dear, for separation from what is dear is bad;
Ganthā tesaṃ na vijjanti, yesaṃ natthi piyāppiyaṃ.
There are no bonds for those for whom there is neither dear nor non-dear.

212. Piyato jāyatī soko, piyato jāyatī bhayaṃ;
From what is dear, grief is born; from what is dear, fear is born;
Piyato vippamuttassa, natthi soko kuto bhayaṃ.
For one who is freed from what is dear, there is no grief; from where would fear come?

213. Pemato jāyatī soko, pemato jāyatī bhayaṃ;
From love, grief is born; from love, fear is born;
Pemato vippamuttassa, natthi soko kuto bhayaṃ.
For one who is freed from love, there is no grief; from where would fear come?

214. Ratiyā jāyatī soko, ratiyā jāyatī bhayaṃ;
From attachment, grief is born; from attachment, fear is born;
Ratiyā vippamuttassa, natthi soko kuto bhayaṃ.
For one who is freed from attachment, there is no grief; from where would fear come?

215. Kāmato jāyatī soko, kāmato jāyatī bhayaṃ;
From sensual craving, grief is born; from sensual craving, fear is born;
Kāmato vippamuttassa, natthi soko kuto bhayaṃ.
For one who is freed from sensual craving, there is no grief; from where would fear come?

216. Taṇhāya jāyatī soko, taṇhāya jāyatī bhayaṃ;
From craving, grief is born; from craving, fear is born;
Taṇhāya vippamuttassa, natthi soko kuto bhayaṃ.
For one who is freed from craving, there is no grief; from where would fear come?

217. Sīladassanasampannaṃ, dhammaṭṭhaṃ saccavedinaṃ;
Endowed with virtue and insight, established in the Dhamma, speaking the truth,
Attano kamma kubbānaṃ, taṃ jano kurute piyaṃ.
Doing one's own kamma—the people hold such a one dear.

218. Chandajāto anakkhāte, manasā ca phuṭo siyā;
One with aspiration for the Indescribable, whose mind is expansive,
Kāmesu ca appaṭibaddhacitto, uddhaṃsototi vuccati.
And whose heart is not bound by sensual pleasures, is called 'one going upstream'.

219. Cirappavāsiṃ purisaṃ, dūrato sotthimāgataṃ;
When a man has been long away and returns safely from afar,
Ñātimittā suhajjā ca, abhinandanti āgataṃ.
Relatives, friends, and well-wishers delight in his arrival.

220. Tatheva katapuññampi, asmā lokā paraṃ gataṃ;
So too, when one who has done merit goes from this world to the next,
Puññāni paṭigaṇhanti, piyaṃ ñātīva āgataṃ.
Their deeds of merit receive them as relatives receive a dear one who has returned.
Piyavaggo soḷasamo niṭṭhito.
The sixteenth chapter is concluded.

221. Kodhaṃ jahe vippajaheyya mānaṃ, saṃyojanaṃ sabbamatikkameyya;
Abandon anger, give up pride, overcome every fetter.
Taṃ nāmarūpasmimasajjamānaṃ, akiñcanaṃ nānupatanti dukkhā.
Suffering does not beset one who is unattached to mind and body, who has nothing.

222. Yo ve uppatitaṃ kodhaṃ, rathaṃ bhantaṃva vāraye;
The one who would restrain anger as it arises, like a careening chariot,
Tamahaṃ sārathiṃ brūmi, rasmiggāho itaro jano.
I proclaim a true charioteer; another person just holds the reins.

223. Akkodhena jine kodhaṃ, asādhuṃ sādhunā jine;
Conquer anger with conciliation, evil with good,
Jine kadariyaṃ dānena, saccenālikavādinaṃ.
Conquer stinginess with a gift, and a liar with the truth.

224. Saccaṃ bhaṇe na kujjheyya, dajjā appampi yācito;
Speak the truth, have no anger, give even if slightly when asked;
Etehi tīhi ṭhānehi, gacche devāna santike.
By these three cases, one would enter the presence of the gods.

225. Ahiṃsakā ye munayo, niccaṃ kāyena saṃvutā;
The harmless sages, always restrained in body,
Te yanti accutaṃ ṭhānaṃ, yattha gantvā na socare.
Go to the unmoving state, where they grieve no more.

226. Sadā jāgaramānānaṃ, ahorattānusikkhinaṃ;
For those who are always awake, learning day and night,
Nibbānaṃ adhimuttānaṃ, atthaṃ gacchanti āsavā.
Intent on Nibbāna, the taints disappear.

227. Porāṇametaṃ atula, netaṃ ajjatanāmiva;
O Atula, this is not new, nor is it only of today:
Nindanti tuṇhimāsīnaṃ, nindanti bahubhāṇinaṃ;
They criticize one who sits silently, they criticize one who talks much,
Mitabhāṇimpi nindanti, natthi loke anindito.
They criticize one who speaks moderately; there is no one in the world who is not criticized.

228. Na cāhu na ca bhavissati, na cetarahi vijjati;
There was not, and there will not be, nor is there now,
Ekantaṃ nindito poso, ekantaṃ vā pasaṃsito.
A person who is wholly criticized or wholly praised.

229. Yaṃ ce viññū pasaṃsanti, anuvicca suve suve;
But the one whom the observant people praise, having closely examined day after day,
Acchiddavuttiṃ medhāviṃ, paññāsīlasamāhitaṃ.
Who is of faultless conduct, intelligent, and endowed with wisdom and virtue—

230. Nikkhaṃ jambonadasseva, ko taṃ ninditumarahati;
Like a coin of fine gold, who is worthy to criticize him?
Devāpi naṃ pasaṃsanti, brahmunāpi pasaṃsito.
Even the gods praise him; even by Brahmā he is praised.

231. Kāyappakopaṃ rakkheyya, kāyena saṃvuto siyā;
Guard yourself from bodily agitation, be restrained in body;
Kāyaduccaritaṃ hitvā, kāyena sucaritaṃ care.
Having abandoned bodily misconduct, act with good conduct in body.

232. Vacīpakopaṃ rakkheyya, vācāya saṃvuto siyā;
Guard yourself from verbal agitation, be restrained in speech;
Vacīduccaritaṃ hitvā, vācāya sucaritaṃ care.
Having abandoned verbal misconduct, act with good conduct in speech.

233. Manopakopaṃ rakkheyya, manasā saṃvuto siyā;
Guard yourself from mental agitation, be restrained in mind;
Manoduccaritaṃ hitvā, manasā sucaritaṃ care.
Having abandoned mental misconduct, act with good conduct in mind.

234. Kāyena saṃvutā dhīrā, atho vācāya saṃvutā;
The steadfast are restrained in body, and restrained in speech,
Manasā saṃvutā dhīrā, te ve suparisaṃvutā.
The steadfast are restrained in mind; they are indeed thoroughly restrained.
Kodhavaggo sattarasamo niṭṭhito.
The Chapter on Anger, the seventeenth, is concluded.

235. Paṇḍupalāsova dānisi, yamapurisāpi ca te upaṭṭhitā;
You are now like a yellowed leaf. Even Yama’s men have come for you.
Uyyogamukhe ca tiṭṭhasi, pātheyyampi ca te na vijjati.
You stand at the mouth of death, With no provisions for the journey.

236. So karohi dīpamattano, khippaṃ vāyama paṇḍito bhava;
Make an island for yourself. Strive quickly. Be wise.
Niddhantamalo anaṅgaṇo, dibbaṃ ariyabhūmiṃ upehisi.
Clear, with impurities removed, You will enter the divine realm of the noble ones.

237. Upanītavayo ca dānisi, sampayātosi yamassa santike;
And now, with life almost finished, You have entered into Yama’s presence.
Vāso te natthi antarā, pātheyyampi ca te na vijjati.
There is no place to rest in between; There are no provisions for the journey.

238. So karohi dīpamattano, khippaṃ vāyama paṇḍito bhava;
Make an island for yourself. Strive quickly. Be wise.
Niddhantamalo anaṅgaṇo, na punaṃ jātijaraṃ upehisi.
Clear, with impurities removed, You will not come to birth and old age again.

239. Anupubbena medhāvī, thokaṃ thokaṃ khaṇe khaṇe;
The wise one, progressively,
Kammāro rajatasseva, niddhame malamattano.
Little-by-little, moment-by-moment, Cleanses his own impurities, like a smith with silver.

240. Ayasāva malaṃ samuṭṭhitaṃ, tatuṭṭhāya tameva khādati;
Just as rust, arisen from iron, eats away at that very iron,
Evaṃ atidhonacārinaṃ, sāni kammāni nayanti duggatiṃ.
So too, the deeds of one who lives excessively lead him to a bad destination.

241. Asajjhāyamalā mantā, anuṭṭhānamalā gharā;
Non-recitation is the stain of hymns; lack of upkeep is the stain of houses;
Malaṃ vaṇṇassa kosajjaṃ, pamādo rakkhato malaṃ.
Laziness is the stain of beauty; negligence is the stain of a guard.

242. Malitthiyā duccaritaṃ, maccheraṃ dadato malaṃ;
Misconduct is the stain of a woman; miserliness is the stain of a donor.
Malā ve pāpakā dhammā, asmiṃ loke paramhi ca.
Stains are evil things, in this world and in the next.

243. Tato malā malataraṃ, avijjā paramaṃ malaṃ;
More impure than these stains is ignorance, the worst of stains.
Etaṃ malaṃ pahantvāna, nimmalā hotha bhikkhavo.
Having abandoned this stain, Monks! be pure.

244. Sujīvaṃ ahirikena, kākasūrena dhaṃsinā;
Easy to live is the life of the shameless, the bold as a crow, the corrupt,
Pakkhandinā pagabbhena, saṃkiliṭṭhena jīvitaṃ.
The pushy, the reckless, and the defiled.

245. Hirīmatā ca dujjīvaṃ, niccaṃ sucigavesinā;
Hard to live is the life of one with conscience, always seeking purity,
Alīnenāppagabbhena, suddhājīvena passatā.
Who is open, careful, clean-living, and sees.

246. Yo pāṇamatipāteti, musāvādañca bhāsati;
Whoever kills a living being, and speaks falsely,
Loke adinnamādiyati, paradārañca gacchati.
Takes what is not given in the world, and goes to another’s wife,

247. Surāmerayapānañca, yo naro anuyuñjati;
And the person who engages in drinking liquor,
Idhevameso lokasmiṃ, mūlaṃ khaṇati attano.
Uproots himself in this very world.

248. Evaṃ bho purisa jānāhi, pāpadhammā asaññatā;
Know this, O person: evil things are of evil character and unrestrained;
Mā taṃ lobho adhammo ca, ciraṃ dukkhāya randhayuṃ.
Do not let greed and injustice subject you to long suffering.

249. Dadāti ve yathāsaddhaṃ, yathāpasādanaṃ jano;
People give according to their faith, according to their trust;
Tattha yo maṅku bhavati, paresaṃ pānabhojane;
The one who is disconcerted by others'food and drink,
Na so divā vā rattiṃ vā, samādhimadhigacchati.
Will never attain samādhi, neither by day nor by night.

250. Yassa cetaṃ samucchinnaṃ, mūlaghaccaṃ samūhataṃ;
But the one for whom this is cut off, destroyed at the root, and removed,
Sa ve divā vā rattiṃ vā, samādhimadhigacchati.
Will indeed attain samādhi, day or night.

251. Natthi rāgasamo aggi, natthi dosasamo gaho;
There is no fire like passion; there is no fetter like ill will;
Natthi mohasamaṃ jālaṃ, natthi taṇhāsamā nadī.
There is no snare like delusion; there is no river like craving.

252. Sudassaṃ vajjamaññesaṃ, attano pana duddasaṃ;
It is easy to see the faults of others, but difficult to see one’s own;
Paresaṃ hi so vajjāni, opunāti yathā bhusaṃ;
One sifts out the faults of others like chaff,
Attano pana chādeti, kaliṃva kitavā saṭho.
But conceals one’s own, as a deceitful gambler conceals a corrupt dice throw.

253. Paravajjānupassissa, niccaṃ ujjhānasaññino;
For the one who sees others'faults, always critical,
Āsavā tassa vaḍḍhanti, ārā so āsavakkhayā.
Toxins only grow; he is far from the destruction of toxins.

254. Ākāseva padaṃ natthi, samaṇo natthi bāhire;
There are no tracks in the sky; the renunciant is not outside;
Papañcābhiratā pajā, nippapañcā tathāgatā.
People delight in impediments; the Tathāgatas are without impediments.

255. Ākāseva padaṃ natthi, samaṇo natthi bāhire;
There are no tracks in the sky; the renunciant is not outside;
Saṅkhārā sassatā natthi, natthi buddhānamiñjitaṃ.
No volitional formations are eternal; there is no agitation for the Buddhas.
Malavaggo aṭṭhārasamo niṭṭhito.
The Chapter on Stains is completed.

256. Na tena hoti dhammaṭṭho, yenatthaṃ sāhasā naye;
One is not firm in Dhamma by hastily assessing what is worthwhile;
Yo ca atthaṃ anatthañca, ubho niccheyya paṇḍito.
The wise one, having investigated both what is worthwhile and what is not, judges them.

257. Asāhasena dhammena, samena nayatī pare;
By non-violence, by Dhamma, he leads others justly;
Dhammassa gutto medhāvī,‘‘ dhammaṭṭho ’’ ti pavuccati.
The wise one who is guarded by the Dhamma is called 'firm in Dhamma'.

258. Na tena paṇḍito hoti, yāvatā bahu bhāsati;
One is not wise just by speaking much;
Khemī averī abhayo,‘‘ paṇḍito ’’ ti pavuccati.
The one who is peaceful, friendly, and fearless is called 'wise'.

259. Na tāvatā dhammadharo, yāvatā bahu bhāsati;
One is not a bearer of Dhamma just by speaking much;
Yo ca appampi sutvāna, dhammaṃ kāyena passati;
But he who, having heard even a little, sees the Dhamma with his own body;
Sa ve dhammadharo hoti, yo dhammaṃ nappamajjati.
He is indeed a bearer of Dhamma, he who does not neglect the Dhamma.

260. Na tena thero so hoti, yenassa palitaṃ siro;
One is not an elder just because his head is gray;
Paripakko vayo tassa,‘‘ moghajiṇṇo ’’ ti vuccati.
He is ripe only in years, and is called 'grown old in vain'.

261. Yamhi saccañca dhammo ca, ahiṃsā saṃyamo damo;
In whom there is truth and Dhamma, harmlessness, self-control, and self-taming;
Sa ve vantamalo dhīro,‘‘ thero ’’ iti pavuccati.
That wise one who has discarded impurities is indeed called 'elder'.

262. Na vākkaraṇamattena, vaṇṇapokkharatāya vā;
Not by mere eloquence or by beauty of complexion,
Sādhurūpo naro hoti, issukī maccharī saṭho.
Does a person become exemplary if he is envious, avaricious, and deceitful.

263. Yassa cetaṃ samucchinnaṃ, mūlaghaccaṃ samūhataṃ;
But in whom this is cut off, destroyed at the root, and removed;
Sa vantadoso medhāvī,‘‘ sādhurūpo ’’ ti vuccati.
That wise one who has discarded ill will is called 'exemplary'.

264. Na muṇḍakena samaṇo, abbato alikaṃ bhaṇaṃ;
One is not a recluse by a bald head if he is without spiritual practice and speaks lies;
Icchālobhasamāpanno, samaṇo kiṃ bhavissati.
Possessed of longing and greed, how will he be a recluse?

265. Yo ca sameti pāpāni, aṇuṃ thūlāni sabbaso;
But he who calms all evils, subtle and gross, completely;
Samitattā hi pāpānaṃ,‘‘ samaṇo ’’ ti pavuccati.
Because he has calmed all evils, he is called 'recluse'.

266. Na tena bhikkhu so hoti, yāvatā bhikkhate pare;
One is not a bhikkhu just by begging from others;
Vissaṃ dhammaṃ samādāya, bhikkhu hoti na tāvatā.
By undertaking a foul tradition, one is not yet a bhikkhu.

267. Yodha puññañca pāpañca, bāhetvā brahmacariyavā;
But he who, having set aside both merit and demerit here, lives the spiritual life;
Saṅkhāya loke carati, sa ve ‘‘ bhikkhū ’’ ti vuccati.
Having reflected, he conducts himself in the world; he is indeed called 'bhikkhu'.

268. Na monena munī hoti, mūḷharūpo aviddasu;
One is not a sage by silence if he is deluded and ignorant;
Yo ca tulaṃva paggayha, varamādāya paṇḍito.
But the wise one who, as if holding a scale, takes up what is best.

269. Pāpāni parivajjeti, sa munī tena so muni;
He avoids evils; for that reason he is a sage;
Yo munāti ubho loke,‘‘ muni ’’ tena pavuccati.
He who understands both worlds is for that reason called 'sage'.

270. Na tena ariyo hoti, yena pāṇāni hiṃsati;
One is not a noble one by the fact that he harms living beings;
Ahiṃsā sabbapāṇānaṃ,‘‘ ariyo ’’ ti pavuccati.
Because of harmlessness to all living beings, one is called 'noble one'.

271. Na sīlabbatamattena, bāhusaccena vā pana;
Not by mere precept and virtue, or even by great learning,
Atha vā samādhilābhena, vivittasayanena vā.
Or by attaining samādhi, or by a solitary dwelling.

272. Phusāmi nekkhammasukhaṃ, aputhujjanasevitaṃ;
“I touch the happiness of renunciation, unpracticed by the ordinary,”
Bhikkhu vissāsamāpādi, appatto āsavakkhayaṃ.
The bhikkhu should not be confident, not having attained the end of defilements.
Dhammaṭṭhavaggo ekūnavīsatimo niṭṭhito.
The nineteenth Chapter on the Firm in Dhamma is concluded.

273. Maggānaṭṭhaṅgiko seṭṭho, saccānaṃ caturo padā;
Of paths, the eightfold is best; of truths, the four proclamations;
Virāgo seṭṭho dhammānaṃ, dvipadānañca cakkhumā.
Of mental states, dispassion is best; and of two-footed beings, the one with vision.

274. Eseva maggo natthañño, dassanassa visuddhiyā;
Only this is the path; there is no other for purity of vision.
Etañhi tumhe paṭipajjatha, mārassetaṃ pamohanaṃ.
Follow this, for it is the bewilderment of Māra.

275. Etañhi tumhe paṭipannā, dukkhassantaṃ karissatha;
Following this, you will make an end of suffering.
Akkhāto vo mayā maggo, aññāya sallakantanaṃ.
The path has been proclaimed by me, having known the removal of the arrow.

276. Tumhehi kiccamātappaṃ, akkhātāro tathāgatā;
The effort must be made by you; the Tathāgatas are only proclaimers.
Paṭipannā pamokkhanti, jhāyino mārabandhanā.
Those practicing and meditative will be freed from Māra's bonds.

277. ‘‘ Sabbe saṅkhārā aniccā ’’ ti, yadā paññāya passati;
“All volitional formations are impermanent,” when one sees with wisdom,
Atha nibbindati dukkhe, esa maggo visuddhiyā.
Then one wearies of suffering; this is the path to purity.

278. ‘‘ Sabbe saṅkhārā dukkhā ’’ ti, yadā paññāya passati;
“All volitional formations are suffering,” when one sees with wisdom,
Atha nibbindati dukkhe, esa maggo visuddhiyā.
Then one wearies of suffering; this is the path to purity.

279. ‘‘ Sabbe dhammā anattā ’’ ti, yadā paññāya passati;
“All things are without self,” when one sees with wisdom,
Atha nibbindati dukkhe, esa maggo visuddhiyā.
Then one wearies of suffering; this is the path to purity.

280. Uṭṭhānakālamhi anuṭṭhahāno, yuvā balī ālasiyaṃ upeto;
Inactive at the time for exertion, young and strong yet slothful,
Saṃsannasaṅkappamano kusīto, paññāya maggaṃ alaso na vindati.
With a mind clinging to resolves, and lazy, the slothful one does not find the path of wisdom.

281. Vācānurakkhī manasā susaṃvuto, kāyena ca nākusalaṃ kayirā;
Guarded in speech, well-restrained in mind, and one should do no unwholesome action with the body;
Ete tayo kammapathe visodhaye, ārādhaye maggamisippaveditaṃ.
One should purify these three paths of action and fulfill the path proclaimed by the sages.

282. Yogā ve jāyatī bhūri, ayogā bhūrisaṅkhayo;
Indeed, from practice wisdom arises; from lack of practice, wisdom is lost;
Etaṃ dvedhāpathaṃ ñatvā, bhavāya vibhavāya ca;
Having known this twofold path of development and decline,
Tathāttānaṃ niveseyya, yathā bhūri pavaḍḍhati.
One should so establish oneself that wisdom increases.

283. Vanaṃ chindatha mā rukkhaṃ, vanato jāyate bhayaṃ;
Cut down the forest (of passion), not just one tree; from the forest, fear is born.
Chetvā vanañca vanathañca, nibbanā hotha bhikkhavo.
Having cut down both the forest and the underbrush, be deforested, O monks.

284. Yāva hi vanatho na chijjati, aṇumattopi narassa nārisu;
For as long as the underbrush of passion is not cut down, even the smallest amount, in a man towards women,
Paṭibaddhamanova tāva so, vaccho khīrapakova mātari.
So long is his mind tethered, like a calf sucking milk from its mother.

285. Ucchinda sinehamattano kumudaṃ sāradikaṃva;
Cut off your own affection, as you would an autumn lotus with your hand;
Santimaggameva brūhaya, nibbānaṃ sugatena desitaṃ.
Cultivate the path of tranquility, Nibbāna, taught by the Well-Gone One.

286. Idha vassaṃ vasissāmi, idha hemantagimhisu;
“Here I will live during the rains, here in winter and summer,”
Iti bālo vicinteti, antarāyaṃ na bujjhati.
Thus the fool thinks; he does not recognize the interruption.

287. Taṃ puttapasusammattaṃ, byāsattamanasaṃ naraṃ;
That man with a mind clinging to sons and cattle,
Suttaṃ gāmaṃ mahoghova, maccu ādāya gacchati.
Death carries away, like a great flood a sleeping village.

288. Na santi puttā tāṇāya, na pitā nāpi bandhavā;
There are no sons for protection, nor father, nor even relatives;
Antakenādhipannassa, natthi ñātīsu tāṇatā.
For one seized by the Ender, there is no protection in kin.

289. Etamatthavasaṃ ñatvā, paṇḍito sīlasaṃvuto;
Having known this rationale, the learned one, restrained by virtue,
Nibbānagamanaṃ maggaṃ, khippameva visodhaye.
Should quickly clear the path leading to Nibbāna.
Maggavaggo vīsatimo niṭṭhito.
The twentieth Chapter on the Path is completed.

290. Mattāsukhapariccāgā, passe ce vipulaṃ sukhaṃ;
If, by renouncing a small happiness, one could see a great happiness,
Caje mattāsukhaṃ dhīro, sampassaṃ vipulaṃ sukhaṃ.
Then let the wise one renounce the small happiness, seeing the great happiness.

291. Paradukkhūpadhānena, attano sukhamicchati;
Whoever seeks his own happiness by causing affliction to others,
Verasaṃsaggasaṃsaṭṭho, verā so na parimuccati.
Is entangled in the bonds of hostility and is not freed from hostility.

292. Yañhi kiccaṃ apaviddhaṃ, akiccaṃ pana kayirati;
For those who leave undone what should be done, and perform what should not be done,
Unnaḷānaṃ pamattānaṃ, tesaṃ vaḍḍhanti āsavā.
For those arrogant and negligent ones, the taints increase.

293. Yesañca susamāraddhā, niccaṃ kāyagatā sati;
But for those whose mindfulness of the body is always properly undertaken,
Akiccaṃ te na sevanti, kicce sātaccakārino;
Who do not practice what should not be done, and are constant in what should be done,
Satānaṃ sampajānānaṃ, atthaṃ gacchanti āsavā.
For those mindful and knowing ones, the taints fit into what is worthwhile.

294. Mātaraṃ pitaraṃ hantvā, rājāno dve ca khattiye;
Having slain mother, father, and two kings among khattiyas,
Raṭṭhaṃ sānucaraṃ hantvā, anīgho yāti brāhmaṇo.
Having slain the kingdom along with its subjects, the brahmin proceeds untroubled.

295. Mātaraṃ pitaraṃ hantvā, rājāno dve ca sotthiye;
Having slain mother, father, and two kings who are learned,
Veyagghapañcamaṃ hantvā, anīgho yāti brāhmaṇo.
Having slain the tiger as the fifth, the brahmin proceeds untroubled.

296. Suppabuddhaṃ pabujjhanti, sadā gotamasāvakā;
The disciples of Gotama always awaken well-awake,
Yesaṃ divā ca ratto ca, niccaṃ buddhagatā sati.
Whose mindfulness is always directed to the Buddha, day and night.

297. Suppabuddhaṃ pabujjhanti, sadā gotamasāvakā;
The disciples of Gotama always awaken well-awake,
Yesaṃ divā ca ratto ca, niccaṃ dhammagatā sati.
Whose mindfulness is always directed to the Dhamma, day and night.

298. Suppabuddhaṃ pabujjhanti, sadā gotamasāvakā;
The disciples of Gotama always awaken well-awake,
Yesaṃ divā ca ratto ca, niccaṃ saṅghagatā sati.
Whose mindfulness is always directed to the Sangha, day and night.

299. Suppabuddhaṃ pabujjhanti, sadā gotamasāvakā;
The disciples of Gotama always awaken well-awake,
Yesaṃ divā ca ratto ca, niccaṃ kāyagatā sati.
Whose mindfulness is always directed to the body, day and night.

300. Suppabuddhaṃ pabujjhanti, sadā gotamasāvakā;
The disciples of Gotama always awaken well-awake,
Yesaṃ divā ca ratto ca, ahiṃsāya rato mano.
Whose mind day and night delights in non-violence.

301. Suppabuddhaṃ pabujjhanti, sadā gotamasāvakā;
The disciples of Gotama always awaken well-awake,
Yesaṃ divā ca ratto ca, bhāvanāya rato mano.
Whose mind day and night delights in development.

302. Duppabbajjaṃ durabhiramaṃ, durāvāsā gharā dukhā;
Going forth is hard, it is hard to enjoy; households are hard to inhabit and painful.
Dukkhosamānasaṃvāso, dukkhānupatitaddhagū;
Living with those unlike oneself is painful; a traveler is befallen by suffering.
Tasmā na caddhagū siyā, na ca dukkhānupatito siyā.
Therefore, one should not be a traveler, nor should one be befallen by suffering.

303. Saddho sīlena sampanno, yasobhogasamappito;
The faithful one, endowed with virtuous behavior, possessed of fame and wealth,
Yaṃ yaṃ padesaṃ bhajati, tattha tattheva pūjito.
Wherever he goes, he is worshipped.

304. Dūre santo pakāsenti, himavantova pabbato;
The good are revealed from afar, like the Himalayas.
Asantettha na dissanti, rattiṃ khittā yathā sarā.
The bad are unseen here, like arrows shot in the night.

305. Ekāsanaṃ ekaseyyaṃ, eko caramatandito;
A single seat, a single bed, wandering alone and untiring,
Eko damayamattānaṃ, vanante ramito siyā.
Alone, mastering himself, one might delight in the forest.
Pakiṇṇakavaggo ekavīsatimo niṭṭhito.
The twenty-first Miscellaneous Chapter is completed.

306. Abhūtavādī nirayaṃ upeti, yo vāpi katvā na karomi cāha;
One who speaks wrongly goes to hell, as does one who, having done a deed, says, “I did not do it.”
Ubhopi te pecca samā bhavanti, nihīnakammā manujā parattha.
Both of these, being of base kamma, are equal after death in the other world.

307. Kāsāvakaṇṭhā bahavo, pāpadhammā asaññatā;
Many with saffron-clad necks are of evil character and unrestrained;
Pāpā pāpehi kammehi, nirayaṃ te upapajjare.
Because of their evil actions, these wicked ones are reborn in hell.

308. Seyyo ayoguḷo bhutto, tatto aggisikhūpamo;
It is better to eat an iron ball, glowing and like a flame of fire,
Yañce bhuñjeyya dussīlo, raṭṭhapiṇḍamasaññato.
Than for an immoral and unrestrained person to eat the country's alms-food.

309. Cattāri ṭhānāni naro pamatto, āpajjati paradārūpasevī;
An immoral man who pursues the wife of another encounters four instances:
Apuññalābhaṃ na nikāmaseyyaṃ, nindaṃ tatīyaṃ nirayaṃ catutthaṃ.
The accumulation of demerit, an unpleasant bed, reproach, and hell as the fourth.

310. Apuññalābho ca gatī ca pāpikā, bhītassa bhītāya ratī ca thokikā;
There is the accumulation of demerit and a bad destination; the delight of a fearful man with a fearful woman is brief,
Rājā ca daṇḍaṃ garukaṃ paṇeti, tasmā naro paradāraṃ na seve.
And the king imposes a harsh punishment; therefore, a man should not pursue another’s wife.

311. Kuso yathā duggahito, hatthamevānukantati;
Just as kusa grass, if wrongly grasped, cuts the hand,
Sāmaññaṃ dupparāmaṭṭhaṃ, nirayāyupakaḍḍhati.
So the spiritual life, if wrongly handled, drags one to hell.

312. Yaṃ kiñci sithilaṃ kammaṃ, saṃkiliṭṭhañca yaṃ vataṃ;
Any action that is lax, and any observance that is impure,
Saṅkassaraṃ brahmacariyaṃ, na taṃ hoti mahapphalaṃ.
A dubious spiritual life—this is not very fruitful.

313. Kayirā ce kayirāthenaṃ, daḷhamenaṃ parakkame;
If one should do something, one should do it firmly;
Sithilo hi paribbājo, bhiyyo ākirate rajaṃ.
For a lax spiritual life only scatters more dust.

314. Akataṃ dukkaṭaṃ seyyo, pacchā tappati dukkaṭaṃ;
An evil action is better not done; an evil action torments one later.
Katañca sukataṃ seyyo, yaṃ katvā nānutappati.
A good action is better done, which, when done, does not cause regret.

315. Nagaraṃ yathā paccantaṃ, guttaṃ santarabāhiraṃ;
Just as a border city is protected both inside and out,
Evaṃ gopetha attānaṃ, khaṇo vo mā upaccagā;
So you should guard yourself; let not a moment escape you;
Khaṇātītā hi socanti, nirayamhi samappitā.
For those who miss the moment grieve when consigned to hell.

316. Alajjitāye lajjanti, lajjitāye na lajjare;
They are ashamed of what is not shameful, and are not ashamed of what is shameful;
Micchādiṭṭhisamādānā, sattā gacchanti duggatiṃ.
Beings who undertake wrong view go to a bad destination.

317. Abhaye bhayadassino, bhaye cābhayadassino;
Seeing fear in the fearless, and seeing no fear in the fearful,
Micchādiṭṭhisamādānā, sattā gacchanti duggatiṃ.
Beings who undertake wrong view go to a bad destination.

318. Avajje vajjamatino, vajje cāvajjadassino;
Imagining fault in the faultless, and seeing no fault in the faulty,
Micchādiṭṭhisamādānā, sattā gacchanti duggatiṃ.
Beings who undertake wrong view go to a bad destination.

319. Vajjañca vajjato ñatvā, avajjañca avajjato;
Having known fault as fault and what is faultless as faultless,
Sammādiṭṭhisamādānā, sattā gacchanti suggatiṃ.
Beings who undertake right view go to a favorable destination.
Nirayavaggo dvāvīsatimo niṭṭhito.
The twenty-second chapter on hell is completed.

320. Ahaṃ nāgova saṅgāme, cāpato patitaṃ saraṃ;
As an elephant in battle endures an arrow shot from a bow,
Ativākyaṃ titikkhissaṃ, dussīlo hi bahujjano.
So shall I endure insult; for the masses are immoral.

321. Dantaṃ nayanti samitiṃ, dantaṃ rājābhirūhati;
It is the tamed one they lead to a gathering; it is the tamed one that the king mounts.
Danto seṭṭho manussesu, yotivākyaṃ titikkhati.
Among humans, the best is the tamed one who endures insult.

322. Varamassatarā dantā, ājānīyā ca sindhavā;
Excellent are tamed mules, thoroughbred horses of Sindh,
