Kuñjarā ca mahānāgā, attadanto tato varaṃ.
And great elephants, tuskers; but more excellent than these is the one who tames himself.

323. Na hi etehi yānehi, gaccheyya agataṃ disaṃ;
For not by these mounts could one go to the unreached region,
Yathāttanā sudantena, danto dantena gacchati.
As one who is tamed goes by means of his tamed self.

324. Dhanapālo nāma kuñjaro, kaṭukabhedano dunnivārayo;
A tusker named Dhanapālaka, rutting and difficult to restrain,
Baddho kabaḷaṃ na bhuñjati, sumarati nāgavanassa kuñjaro.
Bound, does not eat a morsel; the tusker longs for the elephant grove.

325. Middhī yadā hoti mahagghaso ca, niddāyitā samparivattasāyī;
When one is sluggish and gluttonous, a sleeper who lies rolling around,
Mahāvarāhova nivāpapuṭṭho, punappunaṃ gabbhamupeti mando.
Like a great boar nourished on fodder, the ignorant one undergoes rebirth again and again.

326. Idaṃ pure cittamacāri cārikaṃ, yenicchakaṃ yatthakāmaṃ yathāsukhaṃ;
Formerly, this mind went wandering as it wished, according to its desire and pleasure.
Tadajjahaṃ niggahessāmi yoniso, hatthippabhinnaṃ viya aṅkusaggaho.
Today, I will restrain it thoroughly, as an elephant driver subdues a rutting elephant.

327. Appamādaratā hotha, sacittamanurakkhatha;
Be devoted to vigilance; guard your mind.
Duggā uddharathattānaṃ, paṅke sannova kuñjaro.
Raise yourself from the bad path, like a tusker sunk in mud.

328. Sace labhetha nipakaṃ sahāyaṃ, saddhiṃ caraṃ sādhuvihāridhīraṃ;
If you should find an intelligent companion, a fellow-traveler who is wise and virtuous,
Abhibhuyya sabbāni parissayāni, careyya tenattamano satīmā.
Overcoming all dangers, you should journey together, joyful and mindful.

329. No ce labhetha nipakaṃ sahāyaṃ, saddhiṃ caraṃ sādhuvihāridhīraṃ;
If you should not find an intelligent companion, a fellow-traveler who is wise and virtuous,
Rājāva raṭṭhaṃ vijitaṃ pahāya, eko care mātaṅgaraññeva nāgo.
Then journey alone, like a king who has abandoned a conquered kingdom, like the elephant in the Mātaṅga forest.

330. Ekassa caritaṃ seyyo, natthi bāle sahāyatā;
It is better to travel alone; there is no companionship with a fool.
Eko care na ca pāpāni kayirā, appossukko mātaṅgaraññeva nāgo.
Go alone, and do no wrong, wanting little, like the elephant in the Mātaṅga forest.

331. Atthamhi jātamhi sukhā sahāyā, tuṭṭhī sukhā yā itarītarena;
Happy are friends when need arises; happy is contentment in whatever is.
Puññaṃ sukhaṃ jīvitasaṅkhayamhi, sabbassa dukkhassa sukhaṃ pahānaṃ.
Happy is a meritorious deed at the end of life; happy is the abandonment of suffering altogether.

332. Sukhā matteyyatā loke, atho petteyyatā sukhā;
Happy in this world is reverence toward one’s mother, and reverence for one’s father as well.
Sukhā sāmaññatā loke, atho brahmaññatā sukhā.
Happy in this world is reverence toward a renunciant, and reverence for the brahmin.

333. Sukhaṃ yāva jarā sīlaṃ, sukhā saddhā patiṭṭhitā;
Happy is virtue through old age; happy is a firmly established faith.
Sukho paññāya paṭilābho, pāpānaṃ akaraṇaṃ sukhaṃ.
Happy is the attainment of wisdom; happy is doing no wrong.
Nāgavaggo tevīsatimo niṭṭhito.
The twenty-third chapter on the elephant is concluded.

334. Manujassa pamattacārino, taṇhā vaḍḍhati māluvā viya;
The craving of the careless person grows like a creeper.
So plavatī hurā huraṃ, phalamicchaṃva vanasmi vānaro.
He jumps from existence to existence, like a monkey seeking fruit in the forest.

335. Yaṃ esā sahate jammī, taṇhā loke visattikā;
When this miserable craving, this clinging in the world, overwhelms one,
Sokā tassa pavaḍḍhanti, abhivaṭṭhaṃva bīraṇaṃ.
his sorrows grow like bīraṇa grass after rain.

336. Yo cetaṃ sahate jammiṃ, taṇhaṃ loke duraccayaṃ;
But when one overcomes this miserable craving, so difficult to overcome in the world,
Sokā tamhā papatanti, udabinduva pokkharā.
his sorrows fall away like drops of water from a lotus.

337. Taṃ vo vadāmi bhaddaṃ vo, yāvantettha samāgatā;
I say to you: Good fortune to all assembled here!
Taṇhāya mūlaṃ khaṇatha, usīratthova bīraṇaṃ;
Uproot the root of craving, as one would who desires the fragrant root of the bīraṇa grass.
Mā vo naḷaṃva sotova, māro bhañji punappunaṃ.
Let not Māra crush you again and again, as a torrent crushes a reed.

338. Yathāpi mūle anupaddave daḷhe, chinnopi rukkho punareva rūhati;
Just as a tree cut down will grow again when the root remains healthy and strong,
Evampi taṇhānusaye anūhate, nibbattatī dukkhamidaṃ punappunaṃ.
so this suffering arises again and again when dormant craving is not destroyed.

339. Yassa chattiṃsati sotā, manāpasavanā bhusā;
The one in whom the thirty-six streams of craving flow mightily,
Māhā vahanti duddiṭṭhiṃ, saṅkappā rāganissitā.
is carried away by these currents, these thoughts dependent on passion.

340. Savanti sabbadhi sotā, latā uppajja tiṭṭhati;
Streams flow everywhere; the creeper, having sprung up, establishes itself.
Tañca disvā lataṃ jātaṃ, mūlaṃ paññāya chindatha.
Seeing that the creeper has emerged, use wisdom to cut it off at the root.

341. Saritāni sinehitāni ca, somanassāni bhavanti jantuno;
Delights, flowing along and filled with lust, arise in people.
Te sātasitā sukhesino, te ve jātijarūpagā narā.
Surely, those intent on enjoyment, seeking pleasure, undergo birth and old age.

342. Tasiṇāya purakkhatā pajā, parisappanti sasova bandhito;
Encircled by craving, humanity runs around like an ensnared hare.
Saṃyojanasaṅgasattakā, dukkhamupenti punappunaṃ cirāya.
Bound by the fetters of attachment, they undergo long suffering again and again.

343. Tasiṇāya purakkhatā pajā, parisappanti sasova bandhito;
Encircled by craving, humanity runs around like an ensnared hare.
Tasmā tasiṇaṃ vinodaye, ākaṅkhanta virāgamattano.
Therefore, one who desires dispassion for himself should dispel craving.

344. Yo nibbanatho vanādhimutto, vanamutto vanameva dhāvati;
Who, for the sake of being free from the forest (of craving), is resolved upon the forest (of solitude),
Taṃ puggalametha passatha, mutto bandhanameva dhāvati.
and then runs back to the forest (of craving); come and see one freed who runs back into bondage!

345. Na taṃ daḷhaṃ bandhanamāhu dhīrā, yadāyasaṃ dārujapabbajañca;
The fetter made of iron, of wood, or of babbaja grass, is not so strong, say the wise.
Sārattarattā maṇikuṇḍalesu, puttesu dāresu ca yā apekkhā.
But infatuation with jewels and ornaments, or longing for sons and wives, is strong.

346. Etaṃ daḷhaṃ bandhanamāhu dhīrā, ohārinaṃ sithilaṃ duppamuñcaṃ;
That is a strong bond, say the wise, loose, yet difficult to be freed from.
Etampi chetvāna paribbajanti, anapekkhino kāmasukhaṃ pahāya.
When the bond is cut, they go about, free from longing, having abandoned sensual pleasure.

347. Ye rāgarattānupatanti sotaṃ, sayaṃkataṃ makkaṭakova jālaṃ;
Those excited by passion fall into the current, as a spider caught in its self-made web.
Etampi chetvāna vajanti dhīrā, anapekkhino sabbadukkhaṃ pahāya.
Having cut off this passion, the wise set forth, free from longing, having abandoned all suffering.

348. Muñca pure muñca pacchato, majjhe muñca bhavassa pāragū;
Let go of the past, let go of the future, let go of the present; gone to the other shore of existence,
Sabbattha vimuttamānaso, na punaṃ jātijaraṃ upehisi.
with mind everywhere emancipated, you will not again come to birth and old age.

349. Vitakkamathitassa jantuno, tibbarāgassa subhānupassino;
For the one with disturbed thoughts and intense passion, who is focused on pleasant things,
Bhiyyo taṇhā pavaḍḍhati, esa kho daḷhaṃ karoti bandhanaṃ.
craving only grows stronger; indeed, craving fortifies the fetter.

350. Vitakkūpasame ca yo rato, asubhaṃ bhāvayate sadā sato;
For the one devoted to calming thoughts, always mindful, who meditates on what is foul:
Esa kho byanti kāhiti, esa checchati mārabandhanaṃ.
that one will make an end, that one will cut off Māra’s bond.

351. Niṭṭhaṅgato asantāsī, vītataṇho anaṅgaṇo;
One who has arrived at certainty, who is fearless, without craving, and without blemish,
Acchindi bhavasallāni, antimoyaṃ samussayo.
has destroyed the arrows of becoming; this is his final body.

352. Vītataṇho anādāno, niruttipadakovido;
One who is without craving, free of grasping, skilled in the scriptures,
Akkharānaṃ sannipātaṃ, jaññā pubbāparāni ca;
who knows the arrangement of letters, the sequence of before and after,
Sa ve ‘‘ antimasārīro, mahāpañño mahāpuriso ’’ ti vuccati.
such a one is truly called bearing his final body, a great person, possessing great wisdom.

353. Sabbābhibhū sabbavidūhamasmi, sabbesu dhammesu anūpalitto;
I know all; I overcome all. Free of stain in all conditions,
Sabbañjaho taṇhakkhaye vimutto, sayaṃ abhiññāya kamuddiseyyaṃ.
abandoning everything, emancipated by the dissolution of craving, I have understood everything on my own; to whom should I point as teacher?

354. Sabbadānaṃ dhammadānaṃ jināti, sabbarasaṃ dhammaraso jināti;
The gift of Dhamma overwhelms all gifts; the taste of Dhamma overwhelms all tastes.
Sabbaratiṃ dhammarati jināti, taṇhakkhayo sabbadukkhaṃ jināti.
The delight of Dhamma overwhelms all delights; the dissolution of craving overwhelms all suffering.

355. Hananti bhogā dummedhaṃ, no ca pāragavesino;
Wealth wounds those lacking wisdom, not those seeking the other shore.
Bhogataṇhāya dummedho, hanti aññeva attanaṃ.
Craving wealth, those who lack wisdom destroy others, even as themselves.

356. Tiṇadosāni khettāni, rāgadosā ayaṃ pajā;
Fields are ruined by weeds; humanity is ruined by passions.
Tasmā hi vītarāgesu, dinnaṃ hoti mahapphalaṃ.
Therefore, what is given to those whose passions are gone is very fruitful.

357. Tiṇadosāni khettāni, dosadosā ayaṃ pajā;
Fields are ruined by weeds; humanity is ruined by ill will.
Tasmā hi vītadosesu, dinnaṃ hoti mahapphalaṃ.
Therefore, what is given to those whose ill will is gone is very fruitful.

358. Tiṇadosāni khettāni, mohadosā ayaṃ pajā;
Fields are ruined by weeds; humanity is ruined by delusion.
Tasmā hi vītamohesu, dinnaṃ hoti mahapphalaṃ.
Therefore, what is given to those whose delusion is gone is very fruitful.

359. (Tiṇadosāni khettāni, icchādosā ayaṃ pajā;
Fields are ruined by weeds; humanity is ruined by longing.
Tasmā hi vigaticchesu, dinnaṃ hoti mahapphalaṃ.
Therefore, what is given to those whose longing is gone is very fruitful. It does not have (it) in foreign manuscripts, nor is it seen in the commentary.
Tiṇadosāni khettāni, taṇhādosā ayaṃ pajā;
Fields are ruined by weeds; humanity is ruined by craving.
Tasmā hi vītataṇhesu, dinnaṃ hoti mahapphalaṃ.
Therefore, what is given to those whose craving is gone is very fruitful.
Taṇhāvaggo catuvīsatimo niṭṭhito.
The twenty-fourth chapter on craving is concluded.

360. Cakkhunā saṃvaro sādhu, sādhu sotena saṃvaro;
Restraint of the eye is good. Good is restraint of the ear.
Ghānena saṃvaro sādhu, sādhu jivhāya saṃvaro.
Restraint of the nose is good. Good is restraint of the tongue.

361. Kāyena saṃvaro sādhu, sādhu vācāya saṃvaro;
Restraint of the body is good. Good is restraint of speech.
Manasā saṃvaro sādhu, sādhu sabbattha saṃvaro;
Restraint of the mind is good. Good is restraint everywhere.
Sabbattha saṃvuto bhikkhu, sabbadukkhā pamuccati.
The monk, restrained in every way, is released from all suffering.

362. Hatthasaṃyato pādasaṃyato, vācāsaṃyato saṃyatuttamo;
The one restrained in hand, foot, and speech, the fully restrained—
Ajjhattarato samāhito, eko santusito tamāhu bhikkhuṃ.
Who is composed, solitary, and contented, who delights within: this one is truly proclaimed a monk.

363. Yo mukhasaṃyato bhikkhu, mantabhāṇī anuddhato;
The monk who is restrained in speech, who speaks with wisdom, and is unagitated,
Atthaṃ dhammañca dīpeti, madhuraṃ tassa bhāsitaṃ.
Who declares the Dhamma and what is worthwhile—his statement is honey-sweet.

364. Dhammārāmo dhammarato, dhammaṃ anuvicintayaṃ;
Delighting in the Dhamma, devoted to the Dhamma, pondering the Dhamma,
Dhammaṃ anussaraṃ bhikkhu, saddhammā na parihāyati.
Remembering the Dhamma, the monk does not fall from the true Dhamma.

365. Salābhaṃ nātimaññeyya, nāññesaṃ pihayaṃ care;
One should not scorn what one has obtained, nor should one live envying others.
Aññesaṃ pihayaṃ bhikkhu, samādhiṃ nādhigacchati.
The monk who envies others does not attain concentration.

366. Appalābhopi ce bhikkhu, salābhaṃ nātimaññati;
If a monk does not scorn what he has obtained, even if it is little,
Taṃ ve devā pasaṃsanti, suddhājīviṃ atanditaṃ.
Him indeed the devas praise, who lives purely and is unwearied.

367. Sabbaso nāmarūpasmiṃ, yassa natthi mamāyitaṃ;
He who has no sense of mine completely regarding name-and-form,
Asatā ca na socati, sa ve ‘‘ bhikkhū ’’ ti vuccati.
And does not grieve for what is not—he is truly called a monk.

368. Mettāvihārī yo bhikkhu, pasanno buddhasāsane;
The monk who abides in loving-kindness, and is confident in the Buddha’s teaching,
Adhigacche padaṃ santaṃ, saṅkhārūpasamaṃ sukhaṃ.
Would acquire the peaceful state, the quieting of all conditioned things: happiness.

369. Siñca bhikkhu imaṃ nāvaṃ, sittā te lahumessati;
O monk, bail out this boat! Emptied, it will go quickly for you.
Chetvā rāgañca dosañca, tato nibbānamehisi.
Having cut off passion and ill will, you will go to Nibbāna.

370. Pañca chinde pañca jahe, pañca cuttari bhāvaye;
Five one should cut off, five one should abandon, and further five one should develop.
Pañca saṅgātigo bhikkhu,‘‘ oghatiṇṇo ’’ ti vuccati.
The monk who has overcome the five attachments is called one who has crossed over the torrent.

371. Jhāya bhikkhu mā pamādo, mā te kāmaguṇe ramessu cittaṃ;
Meditate, O monk! Do not be negligent! Do not let your mind whirl in sensual pleasure.
Mā lohaguḷaṃ gilī pamatto, mā kandi ‘‘ dukkhamidan ’’ ti ḍayhamāno.
Do not, being negligent, swallow the molten ball, nor cry out as you burn, This is suffering!

372. Natthi jhānaṃ apaññassa, paññā natthi ajhāyato;
There is no meditative depth for the one without wisdom, and no wisdom for the one who does not practice meditation.
Yamhi jhānañca paññā ca, sa ve nibbānasantike.
But one with both jhāna and wisdom is indeed close to Nibbāna.

373. Suññāgāraṃ paviṭṭhassa, santacittassa bhikkhuno;
For the monk who has entered an empty dwelling, with a mind at peace,
Amānusī rati hoti, sammā dhammaṃ vipassato.
There is a superhuman delight in rightly penetrating the Dhamma.

374. Yato yato sammasati, khandhānaṃ udayabbayaṃ;
Whenever one contemplates the rising and falling of the aggregates,
Labhatī pītipāmojjaṃ, amataṃ taṃ vijānataṃ.
One obtains joy and delight. For those who know, this is the Deathless.

375. Tatrāyamādi bhavati, idha paññassa bhikkhuno;
For the wise monk here, this is the beginning:
Indriyagutti santuṭṭhi, pātimokkhe ca saṃvaro.
Guarding one’s senses, contentment, and restraint by the monastic precepts.

376. Mitte bhajassu kalyāṇe, suddhājīve atandite;
Associate with virtuous friends, whose way of life is pure and unwearied.
Paṭisanthāravutyassa, ācārakusalo siyā;
Be of friendly conduct, and be skillful in conduct.
Tato pāmojjabahulo, dukkhassantaṃ karissati.
Thus, filled with joy, you will end suffering.

377. Vassikā viya pupphāni, maddavāni pamuñcati;
Just as the jasmine sheds its withered flowers,
Evaṃ rāgañca dosañca, vippamuñcetha bhikkhavo.
So, O monks, you should shed passion and ill will.

378. Santakāyo santavāco, santavā susamāhito;
The monk who is calm in body, calm in speech, and calm in mind, who is well settled,
Vantalokāmiso bhikkhu,‘‘ upasanto ’’ ti vuccati.
And has cast off worldly gain: he is called at peace.

379. Attanā codayattānaṃ, paṭimaṃsetha attanā;
You yourself ought to exhort yourself; you yourself ought to scrutinize yourself.
So attagutto satimā, sukhaṃ bhikkhu vihāhisi.
Self-guarded and mindful, O monk, you will dwell happily.

380. Attā hi attano nātho,(ko hi nātho paro siyā)
You are your own protector; you are your own guide.
Attā hi attano gati;
You are your own destination.
Tasmā saṃyamamattānaṃ, assaṃ bhadraṃva vāṇijo.
Therefore, restrain yourself as a merchant does a fine horse.

381. Pāmojjabahulo bhikkhu, pasanno buddhasāsane;
The monk, full of joy and pleased in the Buddha’s teaching,
Adhigacche padaṃ santaṃ, saṅkhārūpasamaṃ sukhaṃ.
Would acquire the peaceful state, the quieting of all conditioned things: happiness.

382. Yo have daharo bhikkhu, yuñjati buddhasāsane;
The monk who, even though young, is engaged in the Buddha’s teaching,
Somaṃ lokaṃ pabhāseti, abbhā muttova candimā.
Illumines this world like the moon released from a cloud.
Bhikkhuvaggo pañcavīsatimo niṭṭhito.
The twenty-fifth chapter on monks is concluded.

383. Chinda sotaṃ parakkamma, kāme panuda brāhmaṇa;
Strive, cut off the stream, O brahmin, and drive out sensual craving.
Saṅkhārānaṃ khayaṃ ñatvā, akataññūsi brāhmaṇa.
Knowing the dissolution of conditioned things, you, O brahmin, are a knower of the Uncreated.

384. Yadā dvayesu dhammesu, pāragū hoti brāhmaṇo;
When the brahmin has gone to the other shore by the twofold practice,
Athassa sabbe saṃyogā, atthaṃ gacchanti jānato.
Then for that one who knows, every fetter falls away.

385. Yassa pāraṃ apāraṃ vā, pārāpāraṃ na vijjati;
The one for whom there exists neither this nor the other shore, nor both this and the other shore,
Vītaddaraṃ visaṃyuttaṃ, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
Who is untroubled and unbound: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

386. Jhāyiṃ virajamāsīnaṃ, katakiccamanāsavaṃ;
The pure one who sits absorbed in meditation, who has done what ought to be done, who is without toxins,
Uttamatthamanuppattaṃ, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
Who has reached the highest goal: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

387. Divā tapati ādicco, rattimābhāti candimā;
The sun shines by day, the moon shines by night;
Sannaddho khattiyo tapati, jhāyī tapati brāhmaṇo;
The warrior shines in armor, the brahmin shines in meditation;
Atha sabbamahorattiṃ, buddho tapati tejasā.
But all day and night, the Buddha shines in splendor.

388. Bāhitapāpoti brāhmaṇo, samacariyā samaṇoti vuccati;
One who has warded off evil is a brahmin, one who lives in peaceful conduct is called a renunciant;
Pabbājayamattano malaṃ, tasmā ‘‘ pabbajito ’’ ti vuccati.
One who has driven out one’s own impurities is therefore called 'gone forth'.

389. Na brāhmaṇassa pahareyya, nāssa muñcetha brāhmaṇo;
One should not strike a brahmin, nor should a brahmin give way to anger;
Dhī brāhmaṇassa hantāraṃ, tato dhī yassa muñcati.
Shame on the one who strikes a brahmin, and greater shame on the one who gives way to anger.

390. Na brāhmaṇassetadakiñci seyyo, yadā nisedho manaso piyehi;
For the brahmin, there is nothing better than restraining his mind from what it cherishes;
Yato yato hiṃsamano nivattati, tato tato sammatimeva dukkhaṃ.
Whenever he turns away from the desire to harm, he thereby appeases suffering.

391. Yassa kāyena vācāya, manasā natthi dukkaṭaṃ;
The one who does no wrong in body, speech, and mind,
Saṃvutaṃ tīhi ṭhānehi, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
Who is restrained in these three ways: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

392. Yamhā dhammaṃ vijāneyya, sammāsambuddhadesitaṃ;
You should venerate your teacher by whom you learned the true Dhamma,
Sakkaccaṃ taṃ namasseyya, aggihuttaṃva brāhmaṇo.
Taught by the Perfectly Awakened One, like a brahmin venerates the sacrificial fire.

393. Na jaṭāhi na gottena, na jaccā hoti brāhmaṇo;
Neither by matted hair, nor ancestry, nor birth is one a brahmin;
Yamhi saccañca dhammo ca, so sucī so ca brāhmaṇo.
The one with whom there is the truth and the Dhamma is pure, and that one is a brahmin.

394. Kiṃ te jaṭāhi dummedha, kiṃ te ajinasāṭiyā;
What of your matted hair, O fool? What of your deerskin clothes?
Abbhantaraṃ te gahanaṃ, bāhiraṃ parimajjasi.
You are a tangled thicket within, yet you groom the exterior!

395. Paṃsukūladharaṃ jantuṃ, kisaṃ dhamanisanthataṃ;
The one wearing rags from a dust heap, emaciated, with veins exposed,
Ekaṃ vanasmiṃ jhāyantaṃ, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
Alone in the forest, absorbed in meditation: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

396. Na cāhaṃ brāhmaṇaṃ brūmi, yonijaṃ mattisambhavaṃ;
I do not proclaim a brahmin simply by being born of the womb, sprung from a mother;
Bhovādi nāma so hoti, sace hoti sakiñcano;
If he possesses something he is only called self-important;
Akiñcanaṃ anādānaṃ, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
The one who has nothing, who is free from clinging: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

397. Sabbasaṃyojanaṃ chetvā, yo ve na paritassati;
The one who has cut off every fetter, who indeed trembles not;
Saṅgātigaṃ visaṃyuttaṃ, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
Who has overcome clinging and is unbound: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

398. Chetvā naddhiṃ varattañca, sandānaṃ sahanukkamaṃ;
The one who has cut off the strap and the thong, the cord together with the bridle,
Ukkhittapalighaṃ buddhaṃ, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
Who has lifted the crossbar, the awakened one: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

399. Akkosaṃ vadhabandhañca, aduṭṭho yo titikkhati;
The one who endures insult, binding, and beating without anger,
Khantībalaṃ balānīkaṃ, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
Whose strength is forbearance, an army’s strength: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

400. Akkodhanaṃ vatavantaṃ, sīlavantaṃ anussadaṃ;
The one who is free of anger, who observes the practice, who is virtuous and humble,
Dantaṃ antimasārīraṃ, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
Who is restrained and in the final body: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

401. Vāri pokkharapatteva, āraggeriva sāsapo;
The one who is unsmeared by sensual craving, like water on a lotus leaf,
Yo na limpati kāmesu, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
Or like a mustard seed on the point of an awl: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

402. Yo dukkhassa pajānāti, idheva khayamattano;
The one who realizes the dissolution of suffering right here in this life,
Pannabhāraṃ visaṃyuttaṃ, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
Who has laid down the burden, who is unbound: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

403. Gambhīrapaññaṃ medhāviṃ, maggāmaggassa kovidaṃ;
The one endowed with intelligence and deep knowledge, who knows what is and is not the path,
Uttamatthamanuppattaṃ, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
Who has attained the highest goal: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

404. Asaṃsaṭṭhaṃ gahaṭṭhehi, anāgārehi cūbhayaṃ;
The one who is not given to society with householders or monks,
Anokasārimappicchaṃ, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
Who is homeless and wants little: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

405. Nidhāya daṇḍaṃ bhūtesu, tasesu thāvaresu ca;
The one who has set down the rod toward both the trembling and the firm,
Yo na hanti na ghāteti, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
Who does not kill or cause another to kill: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

406. Aviruddhaṃ viruddhesu, attadaṇḍesu nibbutaṃ;
The one unhindered among the obstructed, cool among those who take up the rod,
Sādānesu anādānaṃ, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
Free from clinging among the attached: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

407. Yassa rāgo ca doso ca, māno makkho ca pātito;
The one for whom passion, ill will, conceit, and contempt have all fallen away,
Sāsaporiva āraggā, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
Like a mustard seed on the point of an awl: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

408. Akakkasaṃ viññāpaniṃ, giraṃ saccamudīraye;
The one who would utter words that are true, instructive, and gentle,
Yāya nābhisaje kañci, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
Words that would anger no one: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

409. Yodha dīghaṃ va rassaṃ vā, aṇuṃ thūlaṃ subhāsubhaṃ;
The one who does not take what is not given here in the world, be it long or short,
Loke adinnaṃ nādiyati, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
Small or large, beautiful or ugly: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

410. Āsā yassa na vijjanti, asmiṃ loke paramhi ca;
The one whose hopes are found neither in this world nor the next,
Nirāsāsaṃ visaṃyuttaṃ, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
Who is independent, unyoked: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

411. Yassālayā na vijjanti, aññāya akathaṃkathī;
The one without attachments, who by knowing is without doubts,
Amatogadhamanuppattaṃ, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
Who has attained immersion into the Deathless: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

412. Yodha puññañca pāpañca, ubho saṅgamupaccagā;
The one who clings not to merit or demerit, who has transcended both,
Asokaṃ virajaṃ suddhaṃ, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
Who is sorrowless, dustless, and pure: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

413. Candaṃva vimalaṃ suddhaṃ, vippasannamanāvilaṃ;
The one who is like the moon, spotless, pure, perfectly clear,
Nandībhavaparikkhīṇaṃ, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
For whom the delight for becoming is exhausted: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

414. Yomaṃ palipathaṃ duggaṃ, saṃsāraṃ mohamaccagā;
The one who has transcended this mire, this bad road, this wandering, and delusion,
Tiṇṇo pāragato jhāyī, anejo akathaṃkathī;
Who has crossed to the other shore, emancipated, who meditates, free from craving and doubt,
Anupādāya nibbuto, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
Without clinging and extinguished: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

415. Yodha kāme pahantvāna, anāgāro paribbaje;
The one who, having renounced sensual desires, would wander about homeless,
Kāmabhavaparikkhīṇaṃ, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
His sensual becoming exhausted: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

416. Yodha taṇhaṃ pahantvāna, anāgāro paribbaje;
The one who, having renounced craving, would wander about homeless,
Taṇhābhavaparikkhīṇaṃ, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
His craving and becoming exhausted: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

417. Hitvā mānusakaṃ yogaṃ, dibbaṃ yogaṃ upaccagā;
The one who has abandoned the human bond and transcended the divine one,
Sabbayogavisaṃyuttaṃ, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
Who is indeed released from all bonds: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

418. Hitvā ratiñca aratiñca, sītibhūtaṃ nirūpadhiṃ;
The one who has left behind attraction and aversion, being tranquil, without acquisitions,
Sabbalokābhibhuṃ vīraṃ, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
A hero, overcoming the whole world: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

419. Cutiṃ yo vedi sattānaṃ, upapattiñca sabbaso;
The one who has realized the dissipation and arising of beings completely,
Asattaṃ sugataṃ buddhaṃ, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
Who is unattached, well-gone, awakened: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

420. Yassa gatiṃ na jānanti, devā gandhabbamānusā;
The one whose course gods, divine musicians, and humans do not know,
Khīṇāsavaṃ arahantaṃ, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
Whose toxins are destroyed, an arahant: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

421. Yassa pure ca pacchā ca, majjhe ca natthi kiñcanaṃ;
The one for whom there is nothing in front, behind, and in between,
Akiñcanaṃ anādānaṃ, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
Who has nothing, who is free from clinging: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

422. Usabhaṃ pavaraṃ vīraṃ, mahesiṃ vijitāvinaṃ;
The excellent one who is a bull, a hero, a great sage, a conqueror, free of craving,
Anejaṃ nhātakaṃ buddhaṃ, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
The one who has taken the bath, who is now awake: that one I proclaim a brahmin.

423. Pubbenivāsaṃ yo vedi, saggāpāyañca passati,
The one who knows his former lives, and sees the heavens and hells,
Atho jātikkhayaṃ patto, abhiññāvosito muni;
And has reached the ending of birth, a sage consummate in direct knowledge,
Sabbavositavosānaṃ, tamahaṃ brūmi brāhmaṇaṃ.
Who has achieved all accomplishments: that one I proclaim a brahmin.
Brāhmaṇavaggo chabbīsatimo niṭṭhito.
The twenty-sixth chapter on Brahmins is concluded.
